seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about the energy transition
seo.transitionTitle: The building energy transition in Luxembourg
seo.transitionContent: Luxembourg has committed to an ambitious energy transition of its building stock. The building sector represents about 35% of national energy consumption. More than 60% of homes are rated D or below.
seo.cpeTitle: The energy passport: classes A to I
seo.cpeContent: The EPC rates buildings from A (very efficient) to I (energy sieve). A class A property sells at 15-30% premium over comparable G-I class.
seo.epbdTitle: The EPBD directive and Luxembourg deadlines
seo.epbdContent: The EU EPBD recast (2024/1275) requires: class E minimum by 2030, class D by 2033, zero-emission by 2050. Luxembourg must renovate 40,000+ homes.
seo.aidesContent: Klimabonus offers: insulation EUR 30-60/m2, renewable heating EUR 2,500-8,000, PV EUR 200-500/kWp. Cumulative with municipal premiums.
seo.faq1q: What is the energy passport (EPC)?
seo.faq1a: A mandatory certificate rating buildings from A to I based on primary energy demand, heating needs and CO2 emissions.
seo.faq2q: What is the EPC's impact on property prices?
seo.faq2a: A class A property sells at 15-30% premium. The green premium is +3-5% per class gained.
seo.faq3q: What are the EPBD deadlines for Luxembourg?
seo.faq3a: Class E minimum by 2030, class D by 2033, zero-emission by 2050.
seo.faq4q: How much does energy renovation cost in Luxembourg?
seo.faq4a: A full renovation costs EUR 30,000-80,000 for a house, partly covered by Klimabonus (up to 50%).
seo.faq5q: How do I get the Klimabonus?
seo.faq5a: Contact Myenergy for free advice, conduct an energy audit, then apply after works to the Ministry of the Environment.

impact.title: Energy class impact on property value
impact.description: Visualize how energy performance influences property prices in Luxembourg.
impact.source: Source: Observatoire de l'Habitat, Spuerkeess analyses
impact.apiConnecting: Connecting to calculation server... First load may take up to 60 seconds.
impact.localFallback: Local calculation — Spring Boot API not connected
impact.explainTitle: What is the green premium / brown discount?
impact.explainGreenText: Energy-efficient properties (classes A to C) sell above the market average. This premium reflects lower heating costs, superior thermal comfort and early compliance with future EPBD standards. In Luxembourg, Observatoire de l'Habitat studies show a premium of up to +8% for A-rated properties relative to the D reference class.
impact.explainBrownText: Conversely, energy-inefficient properties (classes E to I) suffer a discount. Buyers anticipate future renovation costs, rising energy prices and increasing regulatory constraints (progressive rental bans on energy-wasting buildings, EPBD directive 2024). The discount can reach -25% for I-rated properties.
impact.explainMethodText: Adjustment percentages are calibrated from observed transaction price gaps in Luxembourg, segmented by energy passport class. Class D serves as the reference (0% adjustment). Values are consistent with European academic studies and ECB climate risk analyses.
impact.explainSource1: Observatoire de l'Habitat du Luxembourg — Sales price analysis by energy class (2023-2025)
impact.explainSource2: ECB — Climate risk and real estate: emerging evidence on pricing (2024)
impact.explainSource3: EPBD recast Directive 2024/1275 — Zero-emission building stock by 2050
impact.explainSource4: TEGOVA EVS 2025 — ESG & Sustainability in Property Valuation (EVS 5)
impact.fourchetteTip: Variations depend on location, property type and market conditions
impact.calibrationNote: Coefficients calibrated on price differences by energy class observed in Luxembourg (Observatoire de l'Habitat 2025) and European studies (ECB, PriceHubble).

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about energy impact on prices
seo.greenPremiumTitle: Green premium and brown discount: EPC impact on prices
seo.greenPremiumContent: The impact of energy performance on property prices is well documented in Luxembourg. The gap between A-B (green premium: +12-18%) and F-I (brown discount: -15-30%) reaches 25-35% total.
seo.donneesTitle: Municipal data: discounts and premiums by EPC class
seo.donneesContent: Our database aggregates price gaps by EPC class at municipal level based on AED transactions.
seo.etudesContent: Housing Observatory (2023): +3-5% per class. BCL Working Paper (2022): accelerating brown discount since 2020.
seo.strategieContent: Should you renovate to capture the green premium or sell as-is? Our simulator calculates the potential ROI.
seo.faq1q: What is the green premium?
seo.faq1a: The green premium is the added value of energy-efficient properties (class A-B), reaching +12-18% in Luxembourg.
seo.faq2q: What is the brown discount?
seo.faq2a: The brown discount is the price reduction for energy-inefficient properties (class F-I), reaching -15-30%.
seo.faq3q: Is the data reliable?
seo.faq3a: Based on actual AED transactions, corroborated by Housing Observatory and BCL studies.
seo.faq4q: Will the brown discount worsen?
seo.faq4a: Yes, with EPBD deadlines and rising energy costs, the brown discount is expected to increase.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about energy renovation
seo.roiContent: Average ROI for a full renovation (class G to B) is 6-12 years excluding property appreciation.
seo.coutsContent: Roof insulation: EUR 120-220/m2, facade: EUR 150-280/m2, triple glazing: EUR 600-1,200/window, heat pump: EUR 12,000-18,000, VMC: EUR 5,000-10,000.
seo.klimabonusContent: Insulation EUR 30-60/m2, heat pump EUR 2,500-8,000, PV EUR 200-500/kWp, energy audit 50-70% subsidy.
seo.paybackContent: Full renovation (G to B) for 150m2 house: EUR 75,000 cost, EUR 22,000 aids, EUR 6,000/year savings = 8-9 year payback. With green premium, under 5 years.
seo.faq1q: How much does a full energy renovation cost?
seo.faq1a: EUR 60,000-80,000 before aids for a 150m2 house going from G to B.
seo.faq2q: What is the average payback period?
seo.faq2a: 6-12 years excluding property appreciation. Under 5 years including green premium.
seo.faq3q: Do aids cover the full cost?
seo.faq3a: No, Klimabonus covers about 25-40%, municipal aids can bring it to 40-50%.
seo.faq4q: Should I get an energy audit first?
seo.faq4a: Strongly recommended. It identifies priority works and is 50-70% subsidised via Myenergy.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about energy communities
seo.conceptContent: Renewable energy communities (REC) allow citizens, SMEs and local authorities to collectively produce and share renewable electricity, under the law of 3 February 2021.
seo.autoconsommationContent: Self-consumption rates rise from 30-40% (individual) to 50-70% (community of 10-20 members) thanks to consumption profile diversification.
seo.loiContent: Two forms: Renewable Energy Community (REC) and Citizen Energy Community (CEC). Members must be connected to the same distribution network.
seo.economiesContent: Savings of EUR 200-600/year per participating household, through self-consumption savings and surplus feed-in revenue.
seo.faq1q: What is an energy community?
seo.faq1a: A group of citizens, SMEs and/or local authorities who collectively produce and share renewable electricity.
seo.faq2q: How much can I save?
seo.faq2a: EUR 200-600/year per household depending on community size.
seo.faq3q: Do I need to live near the panels?
seo.faq3a: Members must be on the same distribution network, not necessarily the same building.
seo.faq4q: How do I create an energy community?
seo.faq4a: Contact Myenergy, form a participant group, define sharing rules, and declare to the ILR regulator.

epbd.title: EPBD Directive — Timeline & Stranding Risk
epbd.description: The European Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD recast 2024) sets a decarbonisation timeline for the building stock. Select your property's energy class to see the deadlines that apply and assess your stranding risk.
epbd.selectorLabel: Current energy class of your property
epbd.projectionGreenPremium: Green premium projection if class remains unchanged.
epbd.projectionBrownDiscount: Brown discount projection if no renovation is undertaken.
epbd.projectionDisclaimer: Indicative projections based on observed trends and the EPBD timeline. Not financial advice.
epbd.renovationCtaDescription: Estimate the cost, subsidies (Klimabonus, Klimaprêt) and return on investment of an energy renovation.
epbd.source1: Directive (EU) 2024/1275 of 24 April 2024 on the energy performance of buildings (recast)
epbd.source2: Official Journal of the European Union, L 2024/1275, 8 May 2024
epbd.source3: European Commission — Fit for 55, Renovation Wave Strategy
epbd.source4: Ministry of Energy and Spatial Planning, Luxembourg
epbd.milestone2026Detail: Deadline: 29 May 2026. Each member state must transpose the directive into national law.
epbd.milestone2028Detail: All new public buildings must be zero-emission from 2028.
epbd.milestone2030Detail: 16% reduction in primary energy consumption of the worst performing residential buildings.
epbd.milestone2033Detail: 26% reduction in primary energy consumption. Classes E-I must have undergone significant renovations.
epbd.milestone2035Detail: Zero-emission requirement for all new buildings (public and private).
epbd.milestone2040Detail: Total ban on fossil fuel boilers (gas, oil).
epbd.milestone2050Detail: Target: the entire European building stock reaches net zero emissions.

riskLow.description: Your property complies with 2050 targets. Value preserved or enhanced (green premium).
riskLow.action2: Consider improvements to maximise green premium
riskLow.valueImpact: Stable to positive. Growing green premium (+2 to +8% vs class D).

riskModerate.description: Class D: not a 'worst performer', but affected by the fossil boiler ban (2040) and zero-emission target (2050).
riskModerate.action1: Plan a renovation to class B or A before 2035
riskModerate.action2: Replace fossil boiler with a heat pump
riskModerate.action3: Take advantage of Klimabonus while subsidies are available
riskModerate.action4: Anticipate growing discount on mid-range classes
riskModerate.valueImpact: Progressive discount expected. By 2040, -8 to -12% without renovation.

riskHigh.description: Classes E-F: directly affected by the 2030-2033 reduction targets. Risk of non-compliance and significant discount.
riskHigh.action1: Start a deep energy renovation promptly
riskHigh.action2: Target at least class C, ideally B or A
riskHigh.action4: Maximise Klimabonus subsidies (higher rates for bigger class jumps)
riskHigh.valueImpact: Significant discount. By 2033, -10 to -18% without renovation.

riskCritical.description: Classes G-I: 'worst performer' impacted from 2030. Discount already visible and accelerating.
riskCritical.action2: Target class B or A to maximise ROI
riskCritical.action6: If selling: expect aggressive buyer negotiation
riskCritical.valueImpact: Critical discount. Current brown discount -12 to -25%. By 2030, potentially > -30%.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about the EPBD directive
seo.directiveTitle: The EPBD recast directive: implications for Luxembourg
seo.directiveContent: The EPBD recast (2024/1275) sets binding renovation targets. Luxembourg must renovate 40,000+ homes rated F-I by 2033.
seo.echeancesContent: Key deadlines: class E by 2030, class D by 2033, zero-emission by 2050. Transposition into national law by 2026.
seo.strandingContent: Properties in class F-I face increasing risk of value loss and usage restrictions as EPBD deadlines approach.
seo.financementContent: Klimabonus (25-40%), green bank loans, municipal funds, and energy performance contracts.
seo.faq1q: What is the EPBD directive?
seo.faq1a: EU legislation setting minimum energy performance standards for buildings. The 2024 recast imposes binding targets.
seo.faq2q: Will my G-rated property be banned from renting?
seo.faq2a: EPBD requires class E by 2030 and D by 2033. Exact modalities will be set by Luxembourg's transposition.
seo.faq3q: What is stranding risk?
seo.faq3a: The risk that a property loses value or becomes non-compliant, making it difficult to sell or rent without renovation.
seo.faq4q: How can I finance renovation?
seo.faq4a: Via Klimabonus, green loans, municipal aids and energy performance contracts.
seo.faq5q: When must Luxembourg transpose the EPBD?

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about EPC estimation
seo.estimerTitle: Estimating your energy class without a formal audit
seo.estimerContent: Our estimator provides an indicative EPC class based on building characteristics (construction year, heating type, insulation, ventilation). Useful for anticipating a sale or assessing renovation potential.
seo.criteresContent: Construction year, building type, heating system, insulation level, glazing type, and ventilation system.
seo.fiabiliteContent: Accuracy of ±1 class in about 75% of cases. Does not replace an official EPC by an approved Myenergy expert.
seo.apresEstimationTitle: What to do after the estimate
seo.apresEstimationContent: Class A-C: leverage the green premium. Class D-E: targeted works can gain 1-2 classes. Class F-I: renovation recommended, contact Myenergy.
seo.faq1q: Does this replace an official EPC?
seo.faq1a: No, it is indicative only. An official EPC is mandatory for sale or rental.
seo.faq2q: How accurate is the estimator?
seo.faq2a: About ±1 class in 75% of cases.
seo.faq3q: Which criteria matter most?
seo.faq3a: Construction year and heating system are the two most decisive factors.
seo.faq4q: My property is old but renovated — is this considered?
seo.faq4a: Yes, indicate current insulation and heating. Renovations are taken into account.

lenoz.title: Simplified LENOZ Scoring
lenoz.description: LENOZ is Luxembourg's sustainability certification for residential buildings. The official version includes 143 criteria across 6 categories. This simulator offers a simplified grid of approximately 20 key criteria for a quick pre-assessment.
lenoz.disclaimerText: Simplified estimate — the official LENOZ certification requires a full audit by an accredited body (143 criteria). This simulator provides a preliminary indication and does not replace the official process.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about LENOZ certification
seo.certificationContent: LENOZ is Luxembourg's sustainability label for new residential buildings, evaluating 60 criteria across 6 categories. Mandatory for 3% VAT eligibility since 2017.
seo.categoriesContent: Building location, society/economy, energy, ecology, materials, and functionality. Total: 60 points.
seo.notationContent: Platinum (>=48 pts), Gold (>=36), Silver (>=24), Bronze (>=12). LENOZ is mandatory for the 3% super-reduced VAT rate.
seo.avantagesContent: 3% VAT prerequisite, resale value, quality of life, and long-term asset value.
seo.faq1q: What is LENOZ certification?
seo.faq1a: Luxembourg's sustainability label for new residential buildings, covering 60 criteria in 6 categories.
seo.faq2q: Is LENOZ mandatory?
seo.faq2a: Yes, for 3% VAT eligibility on new residential buildings.
seo.faq3q: How much does certification cost?
seo.faq3a: EUR 2,000-5,000 depending on project complexity.
seo.faq4q: What is the difference between LENOZ and EPC?
seo.faq4a: EPC measures energy only. LENOZ is broader: ecology, materials, functionality and location.
seo.faq5a: No, new construction only. An extension to renovations is under discussion.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about property valuation
seo.contextTitle: The Luxembourg real estate market in 2026
seo.contextContent:
The Grand Duchy of Luxembourg's real estate market is going through a post-peak correction that began in late 2022, after more than a decade of nearly uninterrupted growth. According to the Housing Observatory and STATEC, residential prices fell by around 14% between the Q3-2022 peak and Q4-2024, before stabilising in the first half of 2025.

This correction is mainly explained by the ECB monetary tightening (policy rate rising from 0% to 4.50% in 18 months), which reduced household borrowing capacity. Nevertheless, structural fundamentals remain solid: demographic growth of +1.5%/year, 12,000 additional residents in 2024, a dynamic labour market (unemployment rate ~5.6%) and a structurally deficient supply estimated at 3,000 homes per year by the IDEA Foundation.

The Housing Sector Plan (PSL) and the Pacte Logement 2.0 reform aim to accelerate construction, but effects remain slow. The transaction recovery observed since early 2025 (+18% in volume in Q1-2025 vs Q1-2024) confirms that the floor has probably been reached in many municipalities.
seo.methodeTitle: Valuation methods: hedonic, comparables and DCF
seo.methodeContent:
In Luxembourg, three main families of methods coexist for estimating property value:

  • The hedonic method: used by STATEC and the Housing Observatory, it decomposes the price based on objective characteristics (surface area, location, age, energy performance, floor, parking, etc.) via a regression model. Our tool relies on coefficients published by the Observatory and updated quarterly.
  • The comparable method: a classic in property valuation (EVS/TEGoVA standard), it involves adjusting prices from recent transactions on similar properties. Notarial deeds registered with the Registration and Domains Administration (AED) constitute the reference source.
  • The DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) method: mainly used for investment properties, it discounts future net cash flows. Luxembourg institutional investors (SIF/RAIF funds under CSSF supervision) systematically use it for due diligence.

Our estimator combines the hedonic method (for the theoretical price) with recent comparable data (for market anchoring), thus providing a reliable value range.
seo.facteursTitle: Factors influencing property value in Luxembourg
seo.facteursContent:
Many factors affect property values in the Grand Duchy, with variable weightings depending on the property type:

  • Location and municipality: this is the primary factor. Luxembourg City (Belair, Limpertsberg, Kirchberg) shows average prices per m² of EUR 10,000-14,000, compared to EUR 5,000-7,000 in the North (Clervaux, Wiltz). Proximity to transport (tram, CFL station), international schools and employment zones (Kirchberg, Cloche d'Or) creates significant premiums.
  • Surface area and layout: net habitable surface (as defined by the Grand-Ducal regulation of 25 February 1979) remains the dominant quantitative criterion. Price per m² decreases with size (decreasing returns effect).
  • Energy performance (EPC): since the 2010 energy passport reform, the energy class measurably influences prices. A class A property trades at 15-30% more than a class G-I property of comparable surface and location, according to Housing Observatory studies.
  • Condition and renovations: fitted kitchen, modern bathroom, triple glazing, dual-flow VMC and heat pump are valorising elements. Conversely, an asbestos-cement roof or old oil boiler generates discounts.
  • Floor and orientation: for apartments, upper floors with clear views and south/south-west orientation benefit from premiums of 3-8% compared to ground floor.
seo.exempleTitle: Concrete example: valuing an apartment in Esch-sur-Alzette
seo.exempleContent:
Let us take an apartment of 85 m² in Esch-sur-Alzette, Belvaux district, built in 2012, energy class C, with 1 indoor parking space and 1 cellar:

  • Average price per m² in municipality (Q1-2025): ~EUR 6,200 (source: Housing Observatory)
  • Class C adjustment (reference D): +5% → EUR 6,510/m²
  • Age adjustment (2012 vs median 2005): +3% → EUR 6,705/m²
  • Indoor parking: +EUR 25,000 (market flat rate)
  • Gross estimate: 85 × 6,705 + 25,000 = EUR 594,925

Our tool refines this estimate with complete hedonic coefficients and the latest registered comparables, providing an indicative range that you can compare with an expert opinion approved by the Ministry of Housing.

Reminder: an online estimate does not replace a certified valuation (REV/TEGoVA report) required for inheritances, judicial partitions or mortgage guarantees with Luxembourg banks.
seo.faq1q: How accurate is an online property valuation?
seo.faq1a: Online estimators typically achieve an accuracy of ±10-15% compared to the final sale price. Our tool, calibrated on Housing Observatory data, aims for a range of ±10%. For a certified valuation (inheritance, mortgage), consult an approved expert.
seo.faq2q: How does the EPC influence the price in Luxembourg?
seo.faq2a: According to Housing Observatory studies (2023), gaining one energy class represents on average +3 to +5% on the sale price. The premium is more pronounced for houses (higher heating costs) than apartments. Classes A-B benefit from a 'green premium' while classes G-I suffer an increasing 'brown discount'.
seo.faq3q: What data does the estimation tool use?
seo.faq3a: The tool relies on median prices per municipality published by the Housing Observatory (AED source), STATEC hedonic coefficients, and quarterly property price indices. Data is updated each quarter.
seo.faq4q: Should I get a valuation before selling in Luxembourg?
seo.faq4a: Yes, a prior valuation is recommended to set a realistic listing price. A price that is too high extends the selling time (median 90-120 days in Luxembourg) and can lead to more aggressive negotiations. A correctly positioned property sells in an average of 45-60 days.
seo.faq5q: What is the difference between market value and sale price?
seo.faq5a: Market value is the theoretical price an informed buyer would pay under normal market conditions (EVS definition). The actual sale price may deviate depending on the seller's urgency, marketing quality and the buyer's financing conditions. In Luxembourg, the average gap is ±5% in a balanced market.

fraisAcquisition.title: Acquisition Fees Simulator
fraisAcquisition.subtitle: Registration duties, Bëllegen Akt, VAT, notarial fees, mortgage fees
fraisAcquisition.residencePrincipaleHint: Eligible for Bëllegen Akt and 3% VAT
fraisAcquisition.inclureAnnexesHint: Architect, surveyor, diagnostic, moving, buyer agent — for a realistic total project budget.
fraisAcquisition.fraisDiagnosticHint: EPC mandatory since 2009 for existing properties
fraisAcquisition.fraisDemenagementHint: Professional mover — 800 to 3,000 € depending on distance and volume
fraisAcquisition.fraisCourtageHint: If supported by a property hunter (1-3% of the price)
fraisAcquisition.infobellegenText: Tax credit of €40,000 per buyer (€80,000 for a couple) on registration duties. Only applicable for main residence and first use.
fraisAcquisition.infoVefaText: The 7% duties only apply to the land portion. The construction portion is subject to VAT (3% main residence, 17% otherwise), with a tax benefit cap of €50,000.
fraisAcquisition.infoNotaireText: Calculated according to a regulated degressive scale (4% up to €10,000 then degressive).

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about acquisition costs
seo.comprendreTitle: Understanding property acquisition costs in Luxembourg
seo.comprendreContent:
When purchasing property in the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg, the buyer must pay several categories of costs in addition to the purchase price. These acquisition costs, often incorrectly called 'notary fees', represent between 7% and 10% of the property price for existing properties, and can drop to 3-4% for new-build VEFA properties.

The main components are:

  • Registration duties: 6% of the purchase price (existing) or land portion (VEFA), collected by the Registration and Domains Administration (AED).
  • Transcription duty: 1% of the price, for land registry purposes.
  • Notary fees: calculated on a degressive scale set by the Grand-Ducal regulation of 24 September 1987.
  • Mortgage registration fees: 0.05% of the loan amount plus notarial fees on the mortgage deed.
seo.bellegenAktTitle: The Bellegen Akt credit: tax reduction up to EUR 40,000
seo.bellegenAktContent:
The Bellegen Akt (reduced-cost deed) is a major tax advantage granted to buyers of a property intended as their primary residence. Each buyer receives a credit of EUR 20,000 on duties. For a couple buying together, the credit doubles to EUR 40,000. This credit can be used only once in each person's lifetime.

Eligibility conditions:

  • The property must serve as the buyer's primary residence
  • Effective occupation must occur within 2 years of the deed
  • The buyer must not have already used the credit
  • The property must be located in Luxembourg
seo.vefaTvaTitle: VEFA and VAT: the tax regime for new builds
seo.vefaTvaContent:
Purchasing through VEFA (off-plan sale) benefits from a specific tax regime in Luxembourg. The construction portion is subject to VAT (not registration duties), while the land portion remains subject to 7% duties.

Applicable VAT rates:

  • 3% (super-reduced rate): for the buyer's primary residence, subject to conditions, capped at EUR 50,000 in VAT savings.
  • 17% (standard rate): for rental investments or beyond the cap.
seo.exempleTitle: Worked example: EUR 750,000 existing property purchase by a couple
seo.exempleContent:
A typical case: a couple purchases an existing apartment at EUR 750,000 as their primary residence, with a mortgage of EUR 600,000:

  • Registration duties (6%): EUR 45,000
  • Transcription duty (1%): EUR 7,500
  • Gross duties: EUR 52,500
  • Bellegen Akt credit (2 x EUR 20,000): -EUR 40,000
  • Net duties: EUR 12,500
  • Notary fees: ~EUR 6,800
  • Mortgage costs: ~EUR 2,500
  • Total acquisition costs: ~EUR 21,800 (2.9% of price)
seo.faq1q: How much are notary fees in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1a: Notary fees (excluding registration duties) represent about 0.8% to 1.2% of the purchase price for a EUR 750,000 property. Total acquisition costs range from 3% (new-build with Bellegen Akt) to 10% (existing without tax credit).
seo.faq2q: Can the Bellegen Akt be combined with reduced VAT rate?
seo.faq2a: Yes, in VEFA the Bellegen Akt credit applies to registration duties on the land portion, while the reduced VAT rate (3%) applies to the construction portion. Both advantages are cumulative for owner-occupiers.
seo.faq3q: How are registration duties calculated for VEFA?
seo.faq3a: In VEFA, registration duties (6% + 1%) apply only to the land portion declared in the deed. The construction portion is subject to VAT (3% or 17%). The land/construction split must appear in the reservation contract and deed of sale.
seo.faq4q: Can the Bellegen Akt be recovered if I resell?
seo.faq4a: No, the Bellegen Akt credit can only be used once per person. If you resell and repurchase, you cannot benefit from it a second time, except in cases of divorce with judicial partition.
seo.faq5a: The costs are paid when signing the authentic deed at the notary. The notary collects all duties, taxes and fees and transfers them to the relevant authorities. Payment typically occurs 2-3 months after the preliminary sale agreement.

fraisAcquisition.nonResident: I am not a Luxembourg tax resident
fraisAcquisition.nonResidentHint: Cross-border worker FR/BE/DE or resident of another country.
fraisAcquisition.nonResidentDisclaimerText: No Bëllegen Akt or 3 % VAT (both reserved to primary residence in LU). Any future capital gain on resale will be governed by the tax treaty between LU and your country of residence (double-taxation relief). Consult a tax adviser for your specific situation (wealth tax, withholding, treaty).
fraisAcquisition.achatSociete: Purchase via a company (SCI / SARL-S / SARL)
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteHint: Acquisition under the name of a patrimonial real estate company
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteInfoTitle: Tax specifics — purchase via company in Luxembourg
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteItem1: Registration duties: 7 % unchanged, no Bëllegen Akt (reserved to individuals for primary residence) nor 3 % VAT
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteItem2: Rental income: taxed at company level (IRC 18 % + ICC ~6.75 % = ~24.94 % in LU City) then dividend taxed at shareholder level
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteItem3: Capital gains: taxed at IRC without the €50,000 decennial allowance; no half-rate regime
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteItem4: Depreciation: annual allowance deductible (1.5-3 %) — advantage vs individuals
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteItem5: Transfer: share transfer rather than asset transfer = reduced registration duties, but 15 % dividend withholding
fraisAcquisition.achatSocieteExpert: Given the complexity, have the structure validated by an LU accountant or tax adviser. A patrimonial SCI is not always optimal depending on your horizon and family composition.

plusValues.title: Capital Gains Tax Calculator
plusValues.subtitle: Art. 99ter (speculation) and Art. 99bis / 102 LIR — STATEC revaluation coefficients
plusValues.legendeCession: > 2 years: Disposal — half rate + STATEC revaluation
plusValues.infoSpeculationText: Taxed at the full rate (progressive scale). No revaluation of the acquisition price.
plusValues.infoCessionText: Acquisition price revalued using STATEC coefficients. Decennial allowance of €50,000 (€100,000 for couples). Taxed at the half rate.
plusValues.infoResidenceText: Full exemption if effectively and continuously occupied.
plusValues.successionHint: Value used in the succession declaration
plusValues.donationHint: Value used in the notarial deed of donation

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about property capital gains
seo.fiscaliteTitle: Taxation of property capital gains in Luxembourg
seo.fiscaliteContent:
In Luxembourg, property capital gains realised on sale are subject to income tax. The tax regime differs depending on the holding period, pursuant to the amended law of 4 December 1967 on income tax (LIR).

  • Speculation profit (art. 99bis LIR): sale within 2 years — taxed at marginal rate (up to 45.78%)
  • Disposal profit (art. 99ter LIR): sale after 2 years — taxed at half the global rate
seo.speculationContent: When property is sold within 2 years of acquisition, the gain constitutes a speculation profit taxed at the full marginal rate (up to 42% + 7% employment fund contribution = 45.78% in 2025). No standard deduction applies.
seo.cessionContent:
Capital gains realised after more than 2 years benefit from a favourable regime:

  • Acquisition price is revalued using ACD coefficients
  • EUR 50,000 allowance every 10 years (EUR 100,000 for couples)
  • Net gain taxed at half the global rate
seo.exonerationsContent:
Several situations allow total or partial exemption:

  • Primary residence: sale is exempt if the owner resided there continuously since acquisition or for at least 5 years before the sale
  • Inheritance/donation: inheritance uses fair market value at date of death (step-up basis)
  • Expropriation: compensation for public utility is exempt under conditions
seo.faq1q: How is property capital gain calculated in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1a: Capital gain = Sale price - (Revalued acquisition price + acquisition costs + revalued works). The acquisition price is adjusted by ACD coefficients for inflation. A EUR 50,000 allowance is available every 10 years.
seo.faq2q: What is the tax rate on capital gains?
seo.faq2a: Speculation (< 2 years): full marginal rate (up to 45.78%). Disposal (> 2 years): half the global rate, approximately 20-23%. Primary residence is exempt under conditions.
seo.faq3q: How does the EUR 50,000 allowance work?
seo.faq3a: The EUR 50,000 allowance (EUR 100,000 for couples) is granted on disposal profits and renews every 10 years.
seo.faq4q: Is the primary residence exempt?
seo.faq4a: Yes, sale of the primary residence is exempt if you resided there continuously since acquisition or for at least 5 years before the sale (art. 102bis(2) LIR).
seo.faq5q: How do I declare a property capital gain?
seo.faq5a: The gain is declared in the annual tax return (model 100/100F), form 700 for disposal profits. The notary notifies the ACD of every property transaction.

plusValues.reportImpositionHint: I reinvest proceeds in another LU real estate asset
plusValues.reportCond1: Reinvestment in real estate located in LU (or applicable treaties)
plusValues.reportCond2: Deadline: within 2 years of sale (early reinvestment possible under conditions)
plusValues.reportCond3: Cost price of new asset ≥ sale price (otherwise proportional partial deferral)
plusValues.reportCond4: Formal declaration of deferral to the ACD in the IR return
plusValues.reportImpactText: The {montant} tax on the gain is deferred. It will be taxed on the future sale of the reinvested asset, with the updated revaluation coefficient. Major benefit: short-term cash flow gain, inflation protection via revaluation.
plusValues.cessionPartsLabel: Sale of company shares (SCI / SARL-S property company)
plusValues.cessionPartsHint: You sell shares rather than the asset (art. 100 LIR)
plusValues.cessionPartsRegle1: Substantial participation = ≥ 10 % holding during the last 5 years (art. 100 LIR)
plusValues.cessionPartsRegle2: If substantial + > 6 months: half-rate regime (max 23 % instead of 45.78 %)
plusValues.cessionPartsRegle3: If ≤ 6 months: speculation gain, full marginal rate with no abatement

calculLoyer.title: Rent Calculator — Invested Capital
calculLoyer.subtitle: Calculate the maximum legal rent under the law of 21 September 2006 on residential leases in Luxembourg.
calculLoyer.prixAcquisitionHint: Purchase price as stated in the notarial deed
calculLoyer.appliquerVetusteHint: Reduces invested capital based on the property age
calculLoyer.vetusteWarningText: Depreciation is not provided for by the 2006 law but may be invoked by the tenant. Application is at the discretion of the justice of the peace.
calculLoyer.coproToggleHint: Enable to include collective works pro rata of your shares in the invested capital.
calculLoyer.coproTantiemesHint: Share of the unit in common parts (e.g. 100 out of 1,000 thousandths)
calculLoyer.coproTravauxMontantHint: Facade, roof, collective heating, lift, etc.
calculLoyer.coproNote: Only capitalised improvement works (not routine maintenance) can be added to the invested capital. The law of 21.09.2006 (art. 3) allows AGM-voted works that increase rental value.
calculLoyer.historiqueSubtitle: How the maximum legal rent for this unit has evolved according to the lease year (STATEC coefficients applied).
calculLoyer.historiqueNote: STATEC coefficients are published by the Observatoire de la formation des prix. Computation: revalued capital × 5% / 12 months. Evolution reflects monetary revaluation (construction index).
calculLoyer.loi2006Text: The annual rent may not exceed 5% of the invested capital revalued using STATEC revaluation coefficients.
calculLoyer.capitalInvestiText: revalued acquisition price + revalued improvement works.
calculLoyer.reforme2024Text: The threshold was lowered from 5% to 3.5% for dwellings whose invested capital exceeds a certain ceiling. Please check the applicable conditions.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about rent caps
seo.plafonnementTitle: Rent capping in Luxembourg: the legal framework
seo.plafonnementContent:
In Luxembourg, residential rents are regulated by the amended law of 21 September 2006 on residential leases. Article 3 stipulates that annual rent cannot exceed 5% of the invested capital in the property. This cap applies to all residential leases, whether unfurnished or furnished.

The 21 March 2024 reform (law no. 8257) introduced important changes:

  • Reduction of the rate from 5% to 3.5% for properties below a certain revalued capital threshold
  • Strengthened penalties for abusive rents (fines up to EUR 5,000)
  • Obligation to attach detailed invested capital calculation to the lease
seo.capitalInvestiTitle: How to calculate the revalued invested capital
seo.capitalInvestiContent:
The invested capital consists of the acquisition price of the property, revalued using STATEC revaluation coefficients (construction price index), plus the cost of improvement works also revalued.

The formula is:

Invested capital = (Purchase price x Coefficient for purchase year) + (Works x Coefficient for works year)
seo.coefficientContent:
The revaluation coefficients are published by STATEC under the residential construction price index. They are used to update acquisition prices and works costs to determine invested capital under the residential lease law.

  • Base 1960 = 1.00
  • The 2024 coefficient is 8.86 (provisional)
  • Coefficients are revised annually, typically published in March for the previous year
seo.vetusteTitle: Depreciation: an optional but strategic deduction
seo.vetusteContent:
Depreciation is a discount applied to the invested capital to account for wear and obsolescence over time. Luxembourg case law recognises an annual rate of 1% to 2%, capped at 50% maximum.

  • A 30-year-old property with 2%/year depreciation sees its invested capital reduced by 60%, capped at 50%
  • Renovation works 'reset the clock' for the renovated portion
seo.faq1q: How do you calculate the maximum legal rent in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1a: The maximum annual rent is 5% of the revalued invested capital (law of 21 September 2006). Invested capital = acquisition price x STATEC coefficient + revalued works. Our calculator performs this calculation automatically.
seo.faq2q: What are the STATEC revaluation coefficients?
seo.faq2a: These are indices published annually by STATEC (National Institute of Statistics) reflecting the evolution of construction costs since 1960. They update the acquisition price and works costs to calculate invested capital.
seo.faq3q: Has the 5% rate been reduced to 3.5%?
seo.faq3a: The law of 21 March 2024 provides for a reduction to 3.5% for certain properties, but implementation details are still under regulatory discussion. The 5% rate remains applicable pending the Grand-Ducal implementing regulation.
seo.faq4q: Can I apply a surcharge for a furnished property?
seo.faq4a: The law does not provide for a specific surcharge for furnished properties. However, the value of furniture can be included in invested capital if justified by invoices.
seo.faq5q: How can I challenge an excessive rent?
seo.faq5a: The tenant can apply to the justice of the peace under Article 7 of the law of 21 September 2006. The judge can order a rent reduction if it exceeds the legal cap.

calculLoyer.postTravauxToggle: What if I do additional renovations?
calculLoyer.postTravauxIntro: Simulate the impact of a planned renovation on the legal rent cap. The revaluation coefficient of the planned year is applied.
calculLoyer.postTravauxNote: Post-renovation cap only valid if renovations are improvements (energy retrofit Klimabonus, extension, modernization). Routine maintenance does not count. Keep invoices + authorizations.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about buying vs renting
seo.dilemmeTitle: Buying or renting in Luxembourg: the eternal dilemma
seo.dilemmeContent:
The question 'should I buy or rent?' is particularly acute in Luxembourg, where the median apartment price exceeds EUR 620,000 in 2026. The total cost of ownership (TCO) includes not just loan repayments but also acquisition costs (7-10%), maintenance (1-2% of price/year), insurance, property tax and opportunity cost of capital.

Renting, meanwhile, is regulated by the 5% cap on invested capital (law of 21 September 2006). Our simulator compares both scenarios over a customisable horizon.
seo.tcoTitle: Total cost of ownership vs. total cost of renting
seo.tcoContent:
For an objective comparison, our tool calculates the cumulative total cost of both options:

  • Purchase: deposit, acquisition costs, loan payments, insurance, maintenance, property tax, less net capital gain at resale
  • Rental: cumulative rents (indexed to inflation), initial deposit, insurance, plus investment return on savings

The crossover point in Luxembourg typically falls between 7 and 15 years.
seo.levierContent: A major argument for buying is the leverage effect of a mortgage. With a 20% deposit on a EUR 600,000 property, the buyer benefits from capital appreciation on 100% of the property value. Banks in Luxembourg typically finance up to 80-100% (LTV ratio).
seo.seuilContent:
The break-even point depends on many factors:

  • Expected stay duration: below 5-7 years, renting is almost always cheaper due to high acquisition costs
  • Interest rates: low rates (< 3%) favour buying; high rates (> 5%) favour renting
  • Price evolution: appreciation > 2%/year significantly shortens the crossover point
seo.faq1q: How long must I hold a property for buying to be worthwhile?
seo.faq1a: In Luxembourg, the break-even typically falls between 7 and 15 years. With the Bellegen Akt (reducing costs to ~3%), this can drop to 5-7 years.
seo.faq2q: Are acquisition costs included in the comparison?
seo.faq2a: Yes, our tool includes registration duties, transcription, notary fees and mortgage costs (7-10% of price).
seo.faq3q: What alternative return should I use for savings?
seo.faq3a: The alternative return represents what you would earn investing your deposit. A 4-6% return is reasonable for a diversified long-term portfolio.
seo.faq4q: Is buying always better in Luxembourg?
seo.faq4a: Not necessarily. If you plan to stay less than 5 years, rates are high, or you can get a very favourable rent, renting may be more cost-effective.
seo.faq5q: How is the mortgage interest tax deduction integrated?
seo.faq5a: In Luxembourg, mortgage interest is deductible from taxable income for primary residences (cap of EUR 1,500-3,000 depending on loan duration). Our simulator integrates this in the net purchase cost calculation.

simulateurAides.aidesCommunalesText: Aid varies considerably from one municipality to another. Here are some examples:
simulateurAides.communeLuxVille: Acquisition premium up to €30,000, rental assistance
simulateurAides.communeBeckerich: Energy model municipality — specific Klimabonus+ premiums
simulateurAides.contactCommune: Contact your municipal administration to find out the exact aid you may be entitled to.
simulateurAides.cumulText: State and municipal aid can generally be combined, up to an overall ceiling (as a rule, the total cannot exceed the total cost of the project).
simulateurAides.topupSocialHint: x1.5 increase of Klimabonus grants if taxable income ≤ €60,000/year (new 2026)

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about housing aid
seo.panoramaTitle: Overview of housing aid in Luxembourg in 2026
seo.panoramaContent:
The Grand Duchy of Luxembourg offers a comprehensive range of public housing aid, primarily managed by the Ministry of Housing. These aids aim to facilitate homeownership, support energy renovation and help modest households find decent housing.

  • Acquisition premium (Bellegen Akt): tax credit up to EUR 20,000 per buyer on registration duties
  • Interest subsidy: partial coverage of mortgage interest based on household income
  • Construction/renovation premium: lump-sum aid for primary residence
  • Rent subsidy: monthly contribution for low-income tenants
  • Klimabonus / PRIMe House: energy renovation aid
seo.acquisitionTitle: Acquisition aid: Bellegen Akt, interest subsidy and state guarantee
seo.acquisitionContent:
For first-time buyers, Luxembourg offers an attractive package:

  • Bellegen Akt: EUR 20,000 credit per person (max EUR 40,000 per couple)
  • Interest subsidy: the state covers part of mortgage interest for 10 years
  • State guarantee: the state guarantees up to EUR 100,000 for households with insufficient deposit
seo.renovationTitle: Energy renovation aid: PRIMe House and Klimabonus
seo.renovationContent:
The PRIMe House programme and Klimabonus support energy efficiency works:

  • Thermal insulation: EUR 30-60/m²
  • Heating replacement: EUR 2,500-8,000 for heat pumps or biomass
  • Photovoltaic: EUR 200-500/kWp
  • Energy advice: 50-70% subsidy on approved Myenergy advisor fees
seo.conditionsContent: Most housing aids are subject to income conditions based on household taxable income. Applications are made through the Ministry of Housing (online at guichet.lu) or via Myenergy for energy aids.
seo.faq1q: What are the main home purchase aids in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1a: The main aids are the Bellegen Akt (up to EUR 40,000 tax credit), interest subsidy (partial mortgage interest coverage), and state guarantee (state surety up to EUR 100,000). All require the property to be the primary residence.
seo.faq2q: What is the Klimabonus amount in 2026?
seo.faq2a: The enhanced Klimabonus (since December 2024) provides premiums of EUR 30-60/m² for insulation, EUR 2,500-8,000 for renewable heating, and EUR 200-500/kWp for photovoltaics.
seo.faq3q: How do I apply for rent subsidy?
seo.faq3a: Apply online at guichet.lu or directly to the Ministry of Housing with your lease, salary slips, household composition and tax notice. Processing takes 2-4 months.
seo.faq4q: Are the aids cumulative?
seo.faq4a: Yes, in most cases. The Bellegen Akt, interest subsidy and state guarantee are cumulative. The Klimabonus is compatible with municipal aids but not with other state aids for the same works.
seo.faq5q: Are there specific aids for cross-border workers?
seo.faq5a: The Bellegen Akt is reserved for properties in Luxembourg. Cross-border workers residing abroad cannot access it. However, a cross-border worker becoming a resident can benefit when purchasing their primary residence in Luxembourg.

simulateurAides.lastVerif: Rates verified on {date} (Bëllegen Akt, Klimabonus, guichet.lu premiums)

simulateurAides.memorialNote: Manual curation at each session. Primary source: legilux.public.lu. For comprehensive monitoring, subscribe to Mémorial A RSS feeds or Klima-Agence alerts.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about real estate DCF
seo.analyseTitle: DCF analysis for multi-tenant investment property in Luxembourg
seo.analyseContent:
The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) method is the benchmark approach for valuing multi-tenant investment properties. It projects all net cash flows generated by the building over a defined analysis period (typically 10 years) and discounts them at a rate reflecting the investment risk.

In Luxembourg, DCF is particularly relevant for office buildings (Kirchberg, Cloche d'Or, Gare Centrale), mixed-use properties and residential rentals. Institutional investors (SIF/RAIF real estate funds regulated by the CSSF) systematically use this method for their INREV reporting and acquisition due diligence.

The DCF value breaks down into two components: the present value of net income received during the analysis period, and the discounted terminal value (estimated resale price at the end of the period). The terminal value typically represents 50 to 70% of the total value, underscoring the importance of the exit cap rate selection.
seo.bailParBailTitle: Lease-by-lease analysis: rent, indexation and vacancy
seo.bailParBailContent:
Our multi-tenant DCF tool models each lease individually, enabling granular analysis of the rent roll:

  • Contractual rent: the in-place rent is projected until lease expiry, with contractual indexation applied. In Luxembourg, most commercial leases provide for annual indexation based on the consumer price index (IPCN) published by STATEC.
  • Break option: if the lease includes an early termination option, the model incorporates a non-renewal probability that generates a potential vacancy cash flow.
  • Estimated Rental Value (ERV): upon each lease expiry, the rent is rebased to the estimated market rent. The gap between in-place rent and ERV (reversionary potential) is a key indicator of income under- or over-valuation.
  • Structural vacancy: a vacancy rate is applied to the ERV for reletting periods. In Luxembourg, office vacancy in Luxembourg City was approximately 3.5% in early 2025 (source: JLL Luxembourg).
  • Rent-free and fit-out: rent-free periods and tenant fit-out contributions are modelled as negative cash flows in the renewal year.
seo.tauxTitle: Discount rate and exit cap rate
seo.tauxContent:
The choice of rates is critical to the DCF valuation result:

  • Discount rate: reflects the return required by the investor given the risk. In Luxembourg, discount rates for prime offices range between 5.5% and 6.5% (2025). For residential, they range from 4.5% to 5.5%. These rates are derived using the build-up method: risk-free rate (10-year government bond) + real estate risk premium + liquidity premium + specific premium.
  • Exit cap rate: determines the resale value at the end of the analysis period. It is generally 25 to 50 basis points higher than the initial rate to reflect building ageing. Prime office cap rates in Luxembourg-Kirchberg stood at 4.25% in early 2025 (CBRE).
  • Sensitivity: a 25 bps change in the discount rate can alter the value by 3 to 5%. It is essential to perform a cross-sensitivity analysis (discount rate x exit rate) to calibrate the value range.
seo.irrTitle: IRR (Internal Rate of Return) and sensitivity analysis
seo.irrContent:
The IRR (Internal Rate of Return) is the discount rate that sets the net present value (NPV) of the investment to zero. It is the most widely used performance indicator among institutional investors:

  • Unlevered IRR: asset return independent of financing. It enables like-for-like comparison between projects.
  • IRR on equity (levered): return on equity after debt service. Leverage amplifies the return if the unlevered IRR exceeds the cost of debt. Our tool allows debt modelling (amount, rate) to calculate the equity IRR.
  • Sensitivity analysis: our sensitivity matrix crosses discount rate and exit rate to visualise the impact on DCF value. Green, yellow and red zones help identify favourable and unfavourable scenarios.

For a well-located office building in Luxembourg City with firm long-term leases, a target IRR of 5.5 to 7% is representative in 2025. For value-add assets requiring renovation or reletting, investors target an IRR of 8 to 12%.
seo.faq1Q: What discount rate should I use for a DCF in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: The discount rate depends on the asset class and risk profile. For prime offices in Luxembourg City (Kirchberg, CBD), expect 5.5% to 6.5% in 2026. For residential rental, 4.5% to 5.5%. For secondary or repositioning assets, 7% to 9%. The build-up method (risk-free rate + risk premiums) is the most rigorous way to justify the chosen rate.
seo.faq2Q: What is the difference between cap rate and discount rate?
seo.faq2A: The cap rate (capitalisation rate) is the ratio between stabilised net income and the property value — it reflects the immediate yield. The discount rate is the rate used to discount future cash flows in a DCF — it incorporates rent growth expectations and the overall risk premium. The discount rate is generally 100 to 200 bps higher than the cap rate because it also compensates for time risk.
seo.faq3Q: How do I model structural vacancy?
seo.faq3A: Structural vacancy represents the average reletting period between tenants. In Luxembourg, for prime offices, structural vacancy is around 3 to 6 months (vacancy of 2.5 to 5%). For secondary offices, it can reach 12 months. Our tool applies this vacancy rate to the ERV only during reletting periods identified on a lease-by-lease basis.
seo.faq4Q: What analysis horizon should I use for a real estate DCF?
seo.faq4A: The standard horizon is 10 years, corresponding to the typical commercial lease duration in Luxembourg. For assets with very long leases (15-25 years, common in logistics or public sector), it can be extended to 15 years. A shorter horizon (5-7 years) is used for value-add or opportunistic strategies. The key is that the horizon covers at least one full lease renewal cycle.

dcfMulti.sensitivitySubtitle: DCF value by discount rate and exit cap rate (±2 pp around current assumptions).
dcfMulti.sensitivityNote: Green = value higher / red = lower than central assumption (navy). Standard Argus Enterprise / Forbury practice to gauge robustness.
dcfMulti.waterfallSubtitle: Cashflow split between investors (LP) and sponsor (GP) — 8% preferred return, 80/20 split above. Requires debt > 0.
dcfMulti.waterfallNote: Standard 3-tier model (return of capital → preferred return → 80/20 split). In practice institutional LBO/REIT structures add GP catch-up 100% and multiple hurdles. Adjust per LPA (Limited Partnership Agreement).
dcfMulti.capRateChartSubtitle: DCF value vs. terminal cap rate (4% to 7.5%). The gold dashed line marks the current central assumption.
dcfMulti.capRateChartNote: Argus Enterprise / Forbury standard to visualise terminal value sensitivity. Higher cap rate = lower value (riskier market / higher required yield).
dcfMulti.liquiditySubtitle: Annual NOI, distributable cashflow (NOI − debt service − CAPEX) and cumulative cash available for distribution to investors.
dcfMulti.liquidityNote: Distributable cashflow is the cash actually available to the landlord after debt service and recurring CAPEX. The cumulative curve shows when the vehicle has paid back its initial equity.
dcfMulti.monthlyLeaseSubtitle: Monthly rent breakdown per lease over the first 12 months, with indexation and rent-free periods.
dcfMulti.monthlyLeaseNote: Argus Enterprise / Forbury standard. Contractual rent-free periods appear in italics. Expired lease = greyed cell.
dcfMulti.stressSubtitle: DCF value under adverse scenarios. Green = resilient (< 5 % drop), amber = watch, red = vulnerable.
dcfMulti.stressNote: Standard Argus Enterprise / Forbury practice for investor due diligence. An asset green on all 4 stresses is defensive; red on ≥ 2 = ask for price discount or conditions precedent.

seo.faqTitle: FAQ
seo.prixTitle: Property prices by municipality in Luxembourg
seo.prixContent: The tevaxia price map displays average prices per square metre for each Luxembourg municipality, based on data from the Housing Observatory and STATEC.
seo.lireTitle: How to read the price map
seo.lireContent: Data comes from two sources: transaction prices (notarial deeds) and listing prices (property portals, typically 5-15% higher).
seo.evolutionContent: The Luxembourg market saw exceptional growth from 2015 to 2021, followed by a correction in 2023. Since mid-2024, prices have been stabilising.
seo.communesContent: The most popular municipalities combine accessibility, quality of life and long-term value appreciation.
seo.faq1q: Where does the price data come from?
seo.faq1a: Prices come from the Housing Observatory (Ministry of Housing) and aggregated listing prices from major Luxembourg property portals.
seo.faq2q: How often are prices updated?
seo.faq2a: Transaction data is published quarterly by the Housing Observatory. Listing prices are updated more frequently.
seo.faq3q: Why is there a gap between transaction and listing prices?
seo.faq3a: Listing prices are asking prices set by sellers, typically 5-15% higher than actual negotiated prices.
seo.faq4q: How is the market score calculated?
seo.faq4a: The market score (0-100) combines three factors: municipality attractiveness, transaction volume, and recent price trends.
seo.faq5a: No, prices shown exclude fees. In Luxembourg, acquisition costs are approximately 7-10% for existing properties.

For a variable-rate loan, no fee is generally due. Add the mortgage release costs (500-1,000 €) if the loan is fully repaid.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about mortgage lending in Luxembourg
seo.creditContent:
The Luxembourg mortgage market has significant specificities compared to neighbouring countries. The banking sector is dominated by local players (Spuerkeess/BCEE, BIL, BGL BNP Paribas, Raiffeisen) and international banks (ING, Deutsche Bank) offering differentiated terms.

Interest rates in Luxembourg follow the European Central Bank (ECB) decisions, with a typical bank spread of 80 to 150 basis points above the refinancing rate. In 2025, 10-year fixed rates stand around 3.0 to 3.8%, while variable rates (indexed to 3-month EURIBOR) range from 3.2 to 4.0%.

The CSSF (Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier) regulates lending practices through its circulars and recommendations, notably regarding LTV (Loan-to-Value) ratios and repayment capacity. Since 2020, the Systemic Risk Committee (CRS) has issued macro-prudential recommendations specific to the Luxembourg market.
seo.ltvContent:
The Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio is the ratio between the loan amount and the property value. The CSSF and CRS have issued differentiated LTV cap recommendations:

  • First-time buyers (primary residence): recommended maximum LTV of 100% (full financing possible under conditions). In practice, banks frequently grant LTVs of 90 to 100% to first-time buyers benefiting from the State guarantee.
  • Non-first-time buyers (primary residence): recommended maximum LTV of 90%, i.e. a 10% deposit.
  • Buy-to-let investment: LTV capped at 80%, requiring a minimum 20% deposit. Some banks are more restrictive (75%).
  • Buy-to-let: specific conditions apply, sometimes with 70% LTV thresholds and rate premiums of 20 to 50 bps.

Importantly, the LTV is calculated on the appraised value of the property, not the purchase price. If the purchase price exceeds the appraisal, the bank will use the lower value.
seo.capaciteContent:
Borrowing capacity is determined by the debt-to-income ratio, which should generally not exceed 40% of net household income in Luxembourg. This threshold is more generous than in some neighbouring countries (35% in France, 33% in Belgium).

  • Income considered: net salary, rental income (generally retained at 70-80%), self-employment income (3-year average), pensions.
  • Deducted charges: existing loans, alimony, residual rent if applicable.
  • Residual income: beyond the debt ratio, Luxembourg banks verify a minimum residual income threshold (approximately EUR 1,200/month for a single person, EUR 2,000 for a couple).

Our simulator calculates the maximum admissible monthly payment and derives the maximum borrowable amount based on the chosen duration and rate.
seo.amortissementContent:
Luxembourg banks mainly offer two types of repayment schedules:

  • Constant repayment (fixed annuities): the monthly payment remains fixed throughout the loan term. The interest portion decreases progressively while the principal portion increases. This is the most common scheme in Luxembourg.
  • Linear repayment (constant principal): the principal repayment is identical each month, producing decreasing monthly payments. Interest decreases regularly. This scheme is more advantageous in total interest cost but requires higher initial payments.
  • Bullet (interest-only): only interest is paid during the loan term, with principal repaid in full at maturity. Used mainly for buy-to-let investments with a life insurance policy as collateral.

Our amortisation table details month-by-month the evolution of outstanding principal, the interest/principal split and the total credit cost.
seo.dscrContent:
The Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) is a ratio used by banks to assess a rental property's ability to cover debt service:

DSCR = Net Operating Income / Annual Debt Service

  • A DSCR above 1.20 is generally required by Luxembourg banks for a buy-to-let mortgage.
  • A DSCR of 1.00 means that rents exactly cover repayments — a risky situation in case of vacancy or unforeseen events.
  • A DSCR below 1.00 means the investor must supplement debt service from personal income.

For the calculation, net income includes a normative vacancy allowance (generally 5 to 8%), non-recoverable charges and a maintenance provision. Debt service includes both interest and principal repayment.
seo.faq1Q: Fixed or variable rate: which to choose in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: The choice depends on your risk profile and time horizon. A fixed rate offers the security of a constant monthly payment, recommended if you are borrowing at maximum capacity. A variable rate (EURIBOR + margin) can be more advantageous if rates fall, but exposes you to increases. In Luxembourg, a popular option is the capped variable rate, combining a variable rate with a ceiling. In 2025, the gap between fixed and variable has narrowed, making fixed rates particularly attractive.
seo.faq2Q: What is the minimum deposit to buy in Luxembourg?
seo.faq2A: For first-time buyers (primary residence), 100% financing is theoretically possible thanks to the State guarantee. In practice, banks recommend a deposit of 10 to 20%. For buy-to-let investment, the minimum deposit is 20% (80% LTV). Acquisition costs (registration duties, notary fees, VAT) represent approximately 7 to 10% of the price and are generally not financed — they must be added to the deposit.
seo.faq3Q: How does the Luxembourg State guarantee work?
seo.faq3A: The State housing guarantee (amended law of 25 February 1979) allows first-time buyers to benefit from a public guarantee covering up to 30% of the loan amount, capped at EUR 250,000. This guarantee facilitates obtaining a loan without a personal deposit. Conditions include a household income ceiling and the obligation to occupy the property as a primary residence. Applications are made to the Ministry of Housing via the myguichet.lu portal.
seo.faq4Q: What is the impact of CPE energy class on the mortgage?
seo.faq4A: Following CSSF recommendations on climate risks, Luxembourg banks are progressively integrating energy performance into their lending criteria. A property rated A-B may benefit from preferential conditions (rate reduction of 10 to 30 bps). Conversely, properties rated F-G (energy sieves) undergo enhanced analysis, sometimes with an appraisal discount and provisioning for energy renovation costs.
seo.faq5Q: Can I refinance a mortgage in Luxembourg?
seo.faq5A: Yes, refinancing is possible and can be advantageous if rates have fallen since origination. Costs to anticipate include: the early repayment indemnity (IRA, capped at 6 months' interest or 1% of repaid capital under the Consumer Code), mortgage release fees (approximately EUR 500 to 1,000), and new mortgage registration fees. The profitability calculation depends on the rate differential, outstanding capital and residual duration.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about developer appraisals
seo.faisabiliteTitle: The developer appraisal: feasibility tool for real estate projects
seo.faisabiliteContent:
The developer appraisal (or project balance sheet) is the fundamental tool for any property developer to assess the economic viability of a construction or refurbishment project. In Luxembourg, where construction costs and land prices rank among the highest in Europe, rigorous analysis is essential before any commitment.

The appraisal compares projected revenue (sales turnover or rental income) against all costs (land, construction, fees, financing costs, marketing). The difference constitutes the gross margin, from which the developer deducts remuneration and contingencies.

In Luxembourg, development projects are governed by the law of 22 October 2008 (municipal planning sector) and the Grand-Ducal regulation of 28 July 2011 on development plans. Compliance with the PAG (General Development Plan) and PAP (Specific Development Plan) determines the fundamental project parameters: floor area ratio, building height, setbacks, and affordable housing percentage.
seo.compteAReboursContent:
The residual method (or development appraisal) determines the maximum land value by starting from projected revenue and subtracting all costs:

Maximum land value = Revenue - Construction costs - Fees - Financing costs - Developer margin

  • Revenue is calculated based on sales prices per sqm by unit type (apartments, houses, retail), adjusted for location and finish level.
  • Construction costs include structural works, fit-out, technical lots and civil engineering (see our construction estimator).
  • Fees include the architect (8-12% per OAI standards), engineering consultants, surveyor, technical inspection and safety coordination.
  • Financing costs cover land carrying costs and construction financing (rates of 3 to 5% over 24-36 months).

This method is systematically used by Luxembourg developers to negotiate land acquisition prices.
seo.coutsContent:
The cost structure of a development project in Luxembourg typically breaks down as follows:

  • Land: 25 to 40% of revenue depending on location. In Luxembourg City, land can represent up to 50% of the final sale price. Registration duties (7% for building land) are added to the acquisition price.
  • Construction: 35 to 50% of revenue. Construction costs in Luxembourg range from EUR 2,200 to 3,500/sqm GFA depending on finish level and complexity (STATEC/OAI 2026 data). Construction VAT is 17% (standard rate) or 3% (super-reduced rate for housing, capped at EUR 50,000 per dwelling).
  • Fees and expenses: 12 to 18% of revenue. Includes architect, engineers, notary, marketing (2-4%), insurance (decennial, CAR) and financing costs.
  • Developer margin: target of 8 to 15% of revenue for a standard project. Complex or risky projects justify a higher margin.
seo.margeContent:
Development profitability is measured through several indicators:

  • Margin on revenue: ratio between projected net profit and revenue. A target of 10 to 15% is standard in Luxembourg.
  • Return on equity (ROE): ratio between profit and equity invested. With leverage (bank financing at 60-70%), a developer can target an ROE of 15 to 25%.
  • Equity multiple: ratio between profit and initial investment. A multiple of 1.5x to 2.0x over 24-36 months is considered attractive.

Sensitivity analysis is crucial: a 5% drop in sales prices or a 10% increase in construction costs can wipe out the margin. Our tool allows you to simulate these scenarios and determine breakeven thresholds (minimum sale price, maximum land cost).
seo.faq1Q: What is the typical land-to-construction ratio in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: The ratio varies considerably by location. In Luxembourg City (Belair, Limpertsberg, Kirchberg), land represents 40 to 50% of total project cost. In the periphery (Esch-sur-Alzette, Differdange, Mersch), the ratio drops to 25-35%. In rural areas, land may represent only 15-20%. This ratio is a key feasibility indicator: a land-to-revenue ratio above 40% makes the project vulnerable to market downturns.
seo.faq2Q: Is VAT recoverable on a development project?
seo.faq2A: Yes, a VAT-registered developer recovers all VAT on construction costs, fees and expenses. The sale of new housing is subject to VAT (17% or 3% super-reduced with a EUR 50,000/dwelling cap). For sales of building land by a taxable person, VAT also applies. Conversely, the sale of an existing building is subject to registration duties (7%) and not VAT. The choice of tax regime (VAT vs registration duties) can significantly impact project profitability.
seo.faq3A: Marketing risk is a major concern in property development. In Luxembourg, best practices include: pre-sales (targeting 30-40% reservations before construction start), use of a VEFA contract compliant with the law of 18 April 2008, product diversification (mix of unit sizes and finishes), and a pre-sale condition in the land purchase agreement. Our tool allows simulation of different sales pace scenarios and their impact on cash flow and margin.
seo.faq4Q: What is the average timeline for a development project in Luxembourg?
seo.faq4A: The overall timeline for a development project in Luxembourg is typically 4 to 7 years. Obtaining the PAP (Specific Development Plan) can take 12 to 24 months. The building permit requires an additional 3 to 12 months. Construction itself takes 18 to 30 months for a 20 to 50-unit project. Marketing ideally begins 6 to 12 months before delivery. These long timelines tie up capital and increase financing costs, which our tool incorporates into the project balance sheet.

seo.donneesContent: Our market database aggregates multiple public sources including the Housing Observatory, STATEC, AED and public listings to offer a comprehensive view of the Luxembourg property market.
seo.prixCommunesContent: Luxembourg has 102 municipalities with prices varying by a factor of 2-3. Luxembourg City leads at ~EUR 10,500/m2, premium suburbs at EUR 8,000-10,000/m2, and the North at EUR 4,500-5,500/m2.

seo.faq1a: All data comes from official public sources: Housing Observatory, STATEC, AED and Geoportail.

seo.faq2a: Yes, our database also covers offices, retail, logistics and building land.
