valorisation.rptA_description: Select the annexes to include in the PDF report generation.
valorisation.rptA_8_esg: A8 — ESG factors (A8.1 Energy performance, A8.2 Physical risks, A8.3 Transition risks)
valorisation.evsMarketValueDesc: The estimated amount for which an asset or liability should exchange on the valuation date between a willing buyer and a willing seller in an arm's length transaction, after proper marketing, wherein the parties had each acted knowledgeably, prudently, and without compulsion.
valorisation.evsMarketRentDesc: The estimated amount for which a property should be leased on the valuation date between a willing lessor and a willing lessee on appropriate terms in an arm's length transaction, after proper marketing.
valorisation.evsMLVDesc: The value of the property as determined by a prudent assessor, taking into account the long-term sustainability of the value, normal and local market conditions, current use and appropriate alternative uses, excluding any speculative elements. CRR Art. 4(1)(74).
valorisation.evsInvestmentValueDesc: The value of an asset for a particular investor or class of investors, for identified investment objectives. May differ from market value.
valorisation.evsFairValueDesc: The price that would be received to sell an asset or paid to transfer a liability in an orderly transaction between market participants at the measurement date. Concept of 'highest and best use'.
valorisation.evsEquitableValueDesc: The estimated price for the transfer of an asset between identified, knowledgeable, and willing parties that reflects the respective interests of those parties.
valorisation.metricLoyerAfficheReelFranchises: Headline rent vs effective rent (after incentives)
valorisation.notesResAppartement: In Luxembourg, the residential market is dominated by the comparison method. Rent is capped at 5% of invested capital (2006 law). Vacancy rates are historically very low (<3%).
valorisation.notesResMaison: Comparison data is limited in Luxembourg — the Housing Observatory does not publish detailed statistics for houses due to insufficient cadastral data. Longer marketing period than apartments.
valorisation.notesImmeubleRapport: Multi-unit buildings are valued primarily on yield. Analyse each unit: passing rent vs ERV, remaining lease term, reversion potential. The 5% cap applies per residential unit. If the building includes a commercial component, value each component separately then aggregate.
valorisation.notesBureaux: Luxembourg City: mature office market segmented CBD / Kirchberg / Cloche d'Or / Gare. Prime rents ~50-55 EUR/m²/month at Kirchberg. Structural vacancy ~5-7%. Analyse incentives reducing effective vs headline rent. Commercial leases 3/6/9 or fixed.
valorisation.notesCommerces: Distinguish high street (Grand-Rue, avenue de la Gare) from retail parks and shopping centres. Zone A rent is the benchmark. Structural e-commerce risk on some segments. Analyse tenant effort ratio (rent / turnover) — above 8-10%, non-renewal risk.
valorisation.notesHotellerie: Hotels are valued on earnings capacity (stabilised EBITDA), not rent. Distinguish real estate from the operating business. Luxembourg: institutional/business market, occupancy ~70-75%. ADR rising. Profits method (EVS) recommended.
valorisation.notesLogistique: Luxembourg: limited logistics stock, concentrated on Eurohub South (Bettembourg/Dudelange). Rents ~6-9 EUR/m²/month. Long leases (6-9 years firm). ESG increasingly important (BREEAM, rooftop solar).
valorisation.notesTerrain: In Luxembourg, a 1 to 6 price ratio exists between land in Luxembourg City and the north. Check PAG/PAP: buildable zone, COS, CTV/CTL servitudes. The developer's residual method deducts land value from selling price minus construction costs and margin.
valorisation.esgRisqueClasseFG: Energy class F/G — regulatory risk (future rental ban)
valorisation.esgRisqueClasseDE: Energy class D/E — insufficient energy performance
valorisation.esgRisqueInondable: Flood zone — insurance risk and depreciation
valorisation.esgRisquePollueLabel: Proximity to contaminated site — environmental risk
valorisation.esgRecoRenovPrioritaire: Priority energy renovation — eligible for Klimabonus (up to 62.5% of works)
valorisation.esgRecoRenovBC: Consider renovation to reach energy class B/C
valorisation.adjDateExplication: Cumulative variation since the sale of the comparable. Positive = market has risen since the sale, comparable underestimates current value.
valorisation.narrIntroduction: The subject of this analysis is a {assetType} with an area of {surface} m², located in {localisation}. The valuation is carried out on the basis of the {evsType} in accordance with the European Valuation Standards 2025 (TEGOVA, 10th edition).
valorisation.narrContexteMarche: The average observed price in the municipality is {prix}/m² for existing apartments (source: Housing Observatory, notarial deeds).
valorisation.narrMarcheVolumeSignificatif: The local market shows a significant transaction volume ({nb} transactions in the last period), which provides good reliability for reference data.
valorisation.narrMarcheVolumeLimite: The local market shows a limited transaction volume ({nb} transactions), which calls for caution in interpreting comparables.
valorisation.narrMethodeComparaison: **Comparison method** — The value comes to {valeur}, i.e. {m2}/m².
valorisation.narrPosInLine: in line with the municipal average ({prix}/m²)
valorisation.narrCapNOI: The net operating income amounts to {noi}, capitalised at a rate of {taux}%.
valorisation.narrCapSousLoue: The property is currently under-rented compared to the market — reversion potential exists at lease renewal.
valorisation.narrCapSurLoue: The property is rented above market — a downward reversion risk exists at lease expiry.
valorisation.narrMethodeDCF: **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) method** — The value comes to {valeur}, based on a discount rate of {tauxActu}% and an exit yield of {tauxSortie}%.
valorisation.narrDCFIRR: The internal rate of return (IRR) is {irr}%.
valorisation.narrCoherenceExcellente: The methods converge remarkably, which reinforces the reliability of the estimate.
valorisation.narrCoherenceSatisfaisante: The methods show satisfactory consistency, with the spread remaining within acceptable limits.
valorisation.narrCoherenceEcartSignificatif: A significant spread is observed between the methods. The assumptions should be analysed to identify the source of divergence.
valorisation.narrCoherenceEcartImportant: The large spread between methods calls for in-depth analysis. The assumptions of each approach must be reviewed and justified.
valorisation.narrReconciliation: **Reconciled value** — After weighting the methods, the market value is estimated at **{valeur}**, i.e. {m2}/m².
valorisation.narrMLV: **Mortgage Lending Value (MLV)** — After applying prudential discounts in accordance with CRR Art. 229, the mortgage lending value is {mlv}, i.e. {ratio}% of market value.
valorisation.narrESGClasse: **ESG Factors** — The property has energy class {classe}.
valorisation.narrESGBon: The ESG profile is favourable (score {score}/100, level {niveau}), which is an asset in the current regulatory context.
valorisation.narrESGMoyen: The ESG profile is average (score {score}/100, level {niveau}). Energy renovation works could improve the valuation.
valorisation.narrESGInsuffisant: The ESG profile is insufficient (score {score}/100, level {niveau}). The estimated energy discount is {impact}%. Renovation is recommended.
valorisation.narrReserves: This analysis is provided for indicative purposes based on available public data and user-entered parameters. It does not constitute a property valuation within the meaning of EVS 2025 or the Valuation Charter. For an official valuation, please consult a TEGOVA-certified valuer (REV/TRV).

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about property valuation
seo.evs2025Content:
The European Valuation Standards (EVS) 2025, published by TEGoVA, constitute the reference framework for all professional property valuations in Luxembourg. The CSSF, in its circular 21/773, requires credit institutions to use EVS-compliant valuations for properties accepted as collateral.

In Luxembourg, market value is defined as the most probable price at which a property would sell, at the valuation date, after adequate marketing, between a willing seller and buyer, each acting independently. This definition, aligned with Article 4(1)(76) of the CRR, is essential for banks' LTV ratio calculations.

The valuer must document the valuation date, the purpose of the valuation (mortgage security, transaction, IFRS accounting, succession), any special assumptions and the data sources used. The valuation report must be signed by a qualified valuer — in Luxembourg, there is no protected title, but professional practice relies on REV (Recognised European Valuer) qualifications awarded by TEGoVA.
seo.methodesContent:
Our valuation tool integrates the five valuation approaches recognised by international standards:

  • Comparison method: direct approach based on recent transactions of similar properties. In Luxembourg, we use the AED (Registration Administration) database which records all notarial deeds. Adjustments cover area, location, floor, condition, energy class and outdoor amenities.
  • Capitalisation method: net rental income is capitalised at the yield rate observed in the area. Prime office yields in Luxembourg City (Kirchberg, Cloche d'Or) ranged between 4.0% and 4.5% in early 2025 per JLL and CBRE.
  • DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) method: projection of net flows over 10 years with terminal value. Particularly suited to multi-tenant buildings with leases expiring at different dates. The discount rate is determined by the Luxembourg real estate WACC.
  • Mortgage Lending Value (MLV): prudent value required by the CRR regulation (Article 229) for exposures secured by real estate. It incorporates a prudence discount of 10 to 20% from market value.
  • Term and reversion method: distinguishes between contractual income (term) and expected market income after lease expiry (reversion), capitalised at differentiated rates.
seo.reconciliationContent:
A professional valuer never relies on a single method. Reconciliation involves assigning a weight to each approach based on the reliability of available data and the property type:

  • For a standard apartment in an urban area, comparison receives a predominant weight (60-70%) as transactions are numerous and homogeneous.
  • For an investment property, capitalisation and DCF are preferred (combined weight of 70-80%), with comparison serving as a check.
  • For an atypical property (castle, building land, rural property), the valuer relies more on the residual method or adjusted comparison, with wider confidence intervals.

Our reconciliation algorithm uses a Bayesian weighting model that adjusts weights based on the number of available comparables, value dispersion and rental data depth.
seo.rapportContent:
An EVS 2025-compliant valuation report includes the following elements:

  • Property identification: address, cadastral references (section, parcel number), physical description, easements and real rights registered in the Land Registry.
  • Legal context: co-ownership regime, emphyteutic lease, building rights, planning compliance (PAG/PAP).
  • Local market analysis: price trends, transaction volumes, supply and demand, infrastructure projects (tramway, roads, activity zones).
  • Method application: calculation details, data sources, justification of parameters used (rates, comparables, reference rents).
  • Reconciliation and conclusion: retained value, confidence interval, any reservations, conditions precedent.

The report generated by tevaxia follows this structure and can serve as the basis for a formal appraisal, subject to validation by a qualified valuer.
seo.faq1Q: Who can perform a property valuation in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: In Luxembourg, the title of property expert is not legally protected. However, banks generally require a valuer holding the REV (Recognised European Valuer) qualification from TEGoVA, or a member of a recognised professional organisation. The CSSF recommends that valuations for mortgage collateral be performed by professionals independent of the credit origination process.
seo.faq2Q: Which valuation method to use depending on property type?
seo.faq2A: For an apartment or house, the comparison method is generally the most reliable as transactions are numerous. For a leased office or commercial building, income capitalisation or DCF are preferred. For building land, the residual method (development appraisal) is essential. A professional valuer always uses at least two methods and performs a reconciliation.
seo.faq3Q: What is the difference between market value and Mortgage Lending Value (MLV)?
seo.faq3A: Market value represents the most probable price at the valuation date. The MLV (mortgage lending value) is a prudent, time-stable value that considers the property's lasting characteristics and normal local market conditions. It excludes speculative elements and generally applies a 10 to 20% discount from market value. The MLV is required by the CRR regulation for bank capital calculations.
seo.faq4Q: How often should a property be revalued?
seo.faq4A: For mortgage collateral properties, the CSSF requires revaluation at least every 3 years for residential real estate and every year for commercial real estate (circular 21/773). Under IFRS accounting (IAS 40), investment properties must be revalued at each reporting date if the fair value model is adopted. For successions, the valuation must reflect the value at the date of death.
seo.faq5Q: How much does a property appraisal cost in Luxembourg?
seo.faq5A: Property valuer fees in Luxembourg vary depending on the property's complexity and the valuation purpose. For a standard apartment, expect EUR 1,500 to 3,000 for an EVS-compliant report. For an investment building or commercial property, fees generally range from EUR 3,000 to 8,000. Large portfolio valuations benefit from degressive rates. The tevaxia tool provides an instant pre-valuation, useful before commissioning a formal appraisal.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.q1: Are the free tools really unlimited?
faq.a1: We are in launch phase. All features are open for free to gather feedback from Luxembourg professionals. Pro tier will move to €29/month once the platform stabilizes — early adopters will keep preferential access.
faq.q2: Why create an account if it's already free?
faq.a2: An account lets you save your simulations in the cloud (accessible from any device), export professional PDF reports and customise your profile on generated documents.
faq.a3: Your data is hosted in Europe on secure servers (Supabase). We do not sell or share your information. Without an account, nothing leaves your browser.
faq.q4: When will the Pro tier be available?
faq.a4: The Pro tier is under development. Contact us at contact@tevaxia.lu to be notified at launch or to discuss your specific needs.
faq.q5: Can I use tevaxia for official valuation reports?
faq.a5: The tools provide indicative calculations. The Pro tier will include customisable EVS 2025 compliant reports. For a certified valuation report, a qualified valuer is still required.

cgu.title: Terms of Service
cgu.intro: These Terms of Service (TOS) govern the use of the tevaxia.lu platform and its services. By accessing the site or creating an account, the user accepts these TOS without reservation.

article1.title: 1. Purpose
article1.p1: tevaxia.lu is a Luxembourg platform offering: (a) free public tools (calculators, simulators, hedonic estimator, price map), (b) a professional property valuation module compliant with TEGOVA EVS 2025 standard, and (c) professional software for real estate agencies (CRM), condominium managers (syndic) and hoteliers (PMS).
article1.p2: Professional services are available on subscription, after creating an account and an organisation.

article2.title: 2. Access to the service
article2.p1: Free tools are accessible without registration. Professional modules require the creation of an account (email + password or OAuth provider) and acceptance of these TOS.
article2.p2: The user undertakes to provide accurate information upon registration and to keep credentials confidential. Any activity carried out from the account is presumed to be that of its holder.
article2.p3: The publisher reserves the right to suspend an account in the event of fraudulent, abusive use or use contrary to these TOS.

article3.title: 3. Pricing and payment
article3.p1: Plans and current prices are presented on the Pricing page. Subscriptions are billed monthly or annually, payable in advance via Stripe.
article3.p2: A free trial period may be offered. In the absence of cancellation before the end of the trial, the subscription continues at the displayed tariffs.
article3.p3: In the event of non-payment, access to the service may be suspended after reminder. Data remains recoverable for 30 days after suspension.

article4.title: 4. User undertakings
article4.p1: The user undertakes to use the platform in accordance with Luxembourg and European laws in force, in particular:
article4.li1: not to divert the tools for illegal purposes or purposes contrary to public order;
article4.li2: to respect the intellectual property of displayed content;
article4.li3: not to attempt to circumvent technical limitations, massive scraping or attacks on the infrastructure;
article4.li4: to comply with GDPR when processing personal data of third parties (co-owners, customers, tenants…) entered into the platform.

article5.title: 5. Limitation of liability
article5.p1: The platform is provided "as is". The calculation, simulation and valuation tools provide estimates based on scales and regulations in force at the date of update. They do not constitute personalised legal, tax, financial or accounting advice.
article5.p2: The publisher cannot be held responsible for decisions made by the user on the basis of the results obtained. For important acts (acquisition, disposal, judicial expertise…), it is imperative to call on a qualified professional (notary, lawyer, accountant, certified REV/TRV valuer).
article5.p3: The publisher strives to ensure the availability of the platform 24/7 but does not guarantee any contractual SLA outside specific clause of the subscription plan. Interruptions for maintenance, bug or external cause are possible.

article6.title: 6. Intellectual property
article6.p1: All content, code, interfaces, databases and trademarks displayed on tevaxia.lu are the exclusive property of the publisher or its partners. Any unauthorised reproduction, adaptation, distribution or exploitation is prohibited.
article6.p2: The user retains ownership of the data they enter. They grant the publisher a non-exclusive license for hosting and processing limited to the execution of the service.

article7.title: 7. Personal data
article7.p1: The processing of personal data is described in the Privacy Policy. The publisher acts as controller for the data of account-holding users, and as processor for third-party data entered by professional users into the platform.

article8.title: 8. Duration and termination
article8.p1: Free accounts are concluded for an indefinite period. The user can delete their account at any time from their personal space.
article8.p2: Paid subscriptions can be cancelled at any time with effect at the end of the period already paid, without pro rata refund unless otherwise provided.
article8.p3: In the event of serious breach (fraud, persistent non-payment, attack on the platform), the publisher may terminate the account immediately with notification to the user.

article9.title: 9. Modification of the TOS
article9.p1: The publisher reserves the right to modify these TOS. Any substantial modification will be notified to the user by email at least 30 days before it comes into force. Continued use beyond this period constitutes acceptance.

article10.title: 10. Applicable law and jurisdiction
article10.p1: These TOS are governed by Luxembourg law. Any dispute relating to their interpretation or execution shall fall under the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg, after prior attempt at amicable resolution.

navigation.label: Browsing data:
navigation.text: Technical cookies required for the operation of the site. Google Analytics (with prior consent) for audience measurement: pages visited, visit duration, browser type, anonymised IP address.

compte.label: User account:
compte.text: Email address, name (optional), profession (optional), hashed password. You can create an account via email/password or through a third-party service (Google, LinkedIn).

oauth.label: OAuth login (Google, LinkedIn):
oauth.text: Only your email address and name are transmitted by these services. We do not receive your password, contacts, or any other data from your third-party account. Authentication is managed by Supabase Auth via the OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect protocol.

simulations.label: Simulation data:
simulations.text: Data entered in calculators and simulators (amounts, areas, energy parameters, rental lots, etc.) is stored in your browser localStorage and — if you are signed in — synced to Supabase so you can access it from any device. 500-item cap per free user (10,000 for a Pro plan), 180-day retention from last modification, automatic daily purge thereafter.

profil.label: Valuer profile:
profil.text: Name, company, professional contact details — stored in your browser's localStorage or in Supabase if you are logged in. This data is used to personalise valuation reports.

metier.label: Professional B2B operational data:
metier.text: Professional users (syndics, real estate agencies, hoteliers) enter into the Syndic/CRM/PMS modules data concerning third parties: co-owners (name, lot, IBAN, balance), hotel guests (name, stay dates, address, tokenised card number), CRM contacts (name, email, phone, purchase criteria), real estate mandates. For this data, tevaxia acts as processor under GDPR art. 28: the professional user is the controller and must have an appropriate legal basis (performance of a syndic contract, accommodation contract, real estate mandate).

bancaire.label: PSD2 banking data (Enable Banking connection):
bancaire.text: If you connect a bank account via the Enable Banking PSD2 service (AIS — Account Information Service) for automatic reconciliation, retrieved transactions (label, amount, date, reference) are processed in memory during reconciliation and not stored on our servers beyond the session. The PSD2 link has a maximum duration of 90 days imposed by EU regulation 2015/2366, renewable by your bank via SCA. The JWT private key used to sign requests remains on the tevaxia server, never exposed to the browser.

ocr.label: Invoice OCR & documents — local processing:
ocr.text: PDFs and images uploaded in the invoice OCR module are processed exclusively in your browser (PDF.js for text extraction, Tesseract.js for scan OCR). No file, no image, no extracted text is sent to any third-party AI service (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) nor to our servers. Only the structured data extracted (supplier, amount, IBAN, VAT…) that you explicitly validate is then saved to Supabase.

paiement.label: Payment data (Stripe):
paiement.text: If you subscribe to a paid plan, payment processing (credit card, SEPA) is entirely handled by Stripe Payments Europe Limited (Ireland). tevaxia never receives or stores your full card number: only a Stripe customer ID, the last 4 digits of your card and its expiration date are kept in our database to allow you to manage your subscription.

finalites.title: 3. Purposes of Processing
finalites.fonctionnement: Service operation (calculations, simulations, display of results)
finalites.sauvegarde: Saving simulations and valuations to your account

baseLegale.title: 4. Legal Basis for Processing

contrat.label: Performance of a contract (Art. 6.1.b GDPR):
contrat.text: creation and management of your account, saving your simulations.

baseLegale.retrait: You may withdraw your consent at any time for processing based on this legal basis.

duree.title: 6. Data Retention
duree.compte: Account data: kept until the user deletes their account.
duree.simulations: Simulation data (saved valuations, rental lots): 180 days from last modification, 500-item cap per free account (10,000 for a Pro plan). Automatic daily purge at 03:00 UTC for rows exceeding these thresholds. Local data (localStorage) remain available as long as the browser is not cleared.
duree.liensPartages: Public share links: 90 days by default, duration configurable by the user (1 to 365 days) at creation, with optional view cap.
duree.metier: B2B operational data (co-owners, hotel guests, CRM contacts): retained for the duration of the service contract with the professional user. Specific LU legal retention obligations apply (accounting and invoices 10 years under art. L.16-2 Commercial Code, general meeting minutes 10 years, AML/KYC data 5 years).
duree.bancaire: PSD2 Enable Banking data: bank transactions accessed in memory, not persisted beyond the session. PSD2 link: 90 days maximum (renewable via SCA).
duree.ocrFichiers: Invoice OCR: PDFs/images uploaded are never stored on our servers — 100% browser processing. Only structured data validated by the user is saved.

droits.title: 7. Your Rights
droits.intro: In accordance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the Luxembourg law of 1 August 2018, you have the following rights:
droits.acces: Right of access to your personal data
droits.effacement: Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
droits.portabilite: Right to data portability — a button « Export all my data (JSON) » is available in the « Data & plan » section of your profile
droits.exercer: To exercise these rights, contact us at

techniques.label: Technical cookies (necessary):
techniques.text: essential for the operation of the site (language preferences, session). No consent required.

analytiques.label: Analytical cookies (consent):
analytiques.text: Google Analytics, only set after explicit consent. Enable anonymised audience measurement.

auth.label: Authentication cookies:
auth.text: Supabase session management (login token). Required for user account functionality.

transferts.title: 9. Data Transfers Outside the European Union
transferts.p1: Some of our sub-processors are located in the United States. These transfers are governed by standard contractual clauses approved by the European Commission and/or the EU-US Data Privacy Framework adequacy decision.
transferts.p2: Account and simulation data are hosted by Supabase on servers located in the European Union (AWS eu-central-1, Frankfurt, Germany).

securite.title: 10. Security Measures
securite.intro: We implement the following technical and organisational measures to protect your data:
securite.rls: Row Level Security (RLS) on Supabase: each user can only access their own data
securite.hash: Hashed passwords (never stored in plain text)
securite.clientSide: Local browser processing for invoice OCR (PDF.js + Tesseract.js): no document sent to any external AI service
securite.psd2: PSD2 keys stored in server environment variables, JWT RS256 signature for every bank request

dpo.title: 11. Data Protection Officer / Contact
dpo.text: For any questions regarding the protection of your personal data, you can contact us at

reclamation.title: 12. Right to Lodge a Complaint

comparer.selectHint: Choose 2 or 3 evaluations to compare side by side.
comparer.selectAtLeast2: Select at least 2 evaluations to show the comparison.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about property comparison
seo.methodeTitle: The comparable method in Luxembourg real estate
seo.methodeContent:
The comparable method (or direct comparison approach) is the most intuitive and widely used valuation technique for residential transactions in Luxembourg. It estimates property value based on recent transaction prices of similar properties in the same geographical area.

Property transactions are recorded by the Registration and Domains Administration (AED), and aggregated data is published by the Housing Observatory. The EVS (European Valuation Standards) recommends a minimum of 3 relevant comparables, ideally 5-10.
seo.criteresContent:
Choosing relevant comparables relies on several similarity criteria:

  • Location: same municipality, ideally same neighbourhood. Price gaps between neighbourhoods can reach 20-30%
  • Property type: apartment vs house, number of bedrooms, habitable surface
  • Age: same age bracket (new, < 10 years, 10-30 years, > 30 years)
  • Energy performance: same or adjacent EPC class
  • Transaction date: ideally less than 12 months old
seo.ajustementsContent:
No comparable is perfectly identical. Adjustments are needed:

  • Surface: price/m² adjustment with decreasing returns
  • Condition: +5 to +15% for fully renovated, -10 to -20% for properties needing renovation
  • Floor and orientation: +2 to +5% per upper floor for apartments
  • Parking: EUR 25,000-50,000 per indoor space in Luxembourg City
seo.decisionContent: Property comparison is essential for setting sale prices, purchase negotiations, investor due diligence and judicial appraisals. Our tool lets you compare up to 4 properties simultaneously with normalised metrics.
seo.faq1q: How many comparables are needed for a reliable valuation?
seo.faq1a: EVS/TEGoVA recommends a minimum of 3 relevant comparables, ideally 5-10. In Luxembourg's less liquid markets, 3 high-quality comparables may suffice.
seo.faq2q: What is the source of transaction data?
seo.faq2a: Data comes from the Registration and Domains Administration (AED), aggregated by the Housing Observatory. These are actual notarised deed prices, not advertised agency prices.
seo.faq3q: How do I adjust for energy performance?
seo.faq3a: According to the Observatory (2023), gaining one EPC class represents about +3-5% on price. Apply proportional adjustment based on class difference.
seo.faq4q: Can I compare properties from different municipalities?
seo.faq4a: It's possible but tricky. Price gaps between Luxembourg municipalities are very significant (factor of 2-3). Prefer comparables from the same or similar neighbouring municipalities.

convertisseurSurfaces.title: Surface Area Converter
convertisseurSurfaces.subtitle: Conversion between SCB, SCP, usable area and habitable area — OAI FC.04 and ILNAS 101:2016 standards
convertisseurSurfaces.typeSurfaceHint: Surface from which to calculate the others
convertisseurSurfaces.nbNiveauxHint: Number of above-ground floors of the building
convertisseurSurfaces.mursExterieursHint: Average thickness of facade walls (including insulation)
convertisseurSurfaces.mursPorteursHint: Average thickness of load-bearing walls and shear walls
convertisseurSurfaces.partiesCommunesHint: Halls, stairs, plant rooms — as % of SCP (apartment buildings only)
convertisseurSurfaces.typeBatimentHint: Influences the SCB → SCP ratio (facade perimeter vs area)
convertisseurSurfaces.hauteurMinHint: Minimum ceiling height for habitable surface (ILNAS 101)
convertisseurSurfaces.pctSousHauteurHint: Share of usable area below minimum height (attics, sloped ceilings)
convertisseurSurfaces.prixM2Hint: Average selling price per saleable m²
convertisseurSurfaces.sourcesText: OAI FC.04 (Jan. 2025) — Surface calculation sheets, Order of Architects and Consulting Engineers. ILNAS 101:2016 — Luxembourg standard for habitable surface measurement. ACT — Practical guide to vertical cadastre (Jul. 2023), Administration of Cadastre and Topography. RGD PAG art. 32 — Definition of Gross Built Surface.
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainSectionSubtitle: How many m² can I build on a plot under Luxembourg PAG zoning? Enter the cadastral surface and the zone.
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainZonePagHint: Communal zoning — check geoportail.lu for the exact address
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainRetraitVoirieHint: Area to be ceded for road widening, pavement, etc.
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainEspaceVertHint: % dedicated to green areas / rainwater retention / plantings per communal rules
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainSurfaceLogementHint: To estimate the number of dwellings that fit (floor area / unit)
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainMethod1: Net surface = (cadastral surface − road setback) × (1 − green-space %)
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainMethod2: Footprint = net surface × COS (Ground Occupation Coefficient)
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainMethod3: Floor area = net surface × CMU (Maximum Utilisation Coefficient)
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainMethod4: Number of dwellings = floor area ÷ average unit surface
convertisseurSurfaces.terrainDisclaimer: Indicative estimate based on typical Luxembourg PAG ranges. Actual values are set by the communal PAG/PAP and may include constraints (setback, height, building envelope, servitudes) not modelled here. Consult the commune or an architect before any project.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about property surfaces
seo.comprendreTitle: Understanding property surfaces in Luxembourg: GBA, NFA, usable, habitable
seo.comprendreContent:
In Luxembourg, the multiplicity of surface definitions is a frequent source of confusion for buyers, investors and even professionals. Unlike France where the Carrez law imposes a single definition, the Grand Duchy uses several coexisting frameworks:

  • Gross Built Area (SCB): total area measured to the outside of walls, including walls, technical shafts and common areas. Used for floor area ratio calculations in PAG/PAP plans.
  • Net Construction Area (SCP): area measured inside load-bearing walls, excluding technical shafts but including internal partitions. The reference for construction costs (OAI FC.04).
  • Usable Area (SU): actually usable area, excluding walls, partitions, shafts and internal stairs. The most relevant area for comparing dwellings.
  • Habitable Area: subset of usable area, limited to rooms used for habitation. Excludes cellars, unconverted attics and garages.
seo.normesTitle: Reference standards: OAI FC.04 and ILNAS 101
seo.normesContent:
Two main standards govern surface measurement in Luxembourg:

  • OAI FC.04 (Calculation Sheet No. 4): published by the Order of Architects and Consulting Engineers, it defines measurement methods for construction projects. It distinguishes SCB, SCP, usable area and ancillary areas. This is the reference used by architects for plans and fee calculations.
  • ILNAS-EN 15221-6: European standard transposed in Luxembourg by ILNAS. It harmonises surface definitions for facility management, distinguishing Gross Floor Area (GFA), Net Floor Area (NFA) and Net Room Area (NRA).

The correspondence between the two frameworks is not always direct, requiring conversion tables. Our tool integrates the official conversion ratios published by the OAI.
seo.ponderationTitle: Weighting of ancillary surfaces: cellars, balconies, terraces, garages
seo.ponderationContent:
In Luxembourg property practice, ancillary surfaces are not counted at 100% of their value. Standard weighting coefficients are:

  • Cellars: coefficient of 0.25 to 0.50 depending on ceiling height and fit-out. A converted cellar (height > 2.20m, natural light) is weighted at 0.50.
  • Balconies: coefficient of 0.30 to 0.50. A covered balcony is weighted higher (0.50) than an open one (0.30).
  • Terraces: coefficient of 0.25 to 0.50 depending on area and aspect. Large penthouse terraces can be weighted at 0.50.
  • Private gardens: coefficient of 0.10 to 0.20 depending on location and area.
  • Garages and parking: not weighted in habitable area, but valued separately (EUR 25,000 to 50,000 per indoor space).

These coefficients are used to calculate the weighted area that serves as the basis for price per sqm in transactions and valuations.
seo.circulaireTitle: Tax administration guidelines on commercial surfaces
seo.circulaireContent:
The Direct Tax Administration (ACD) and the Registration Administration (AED) use specific surface definitions for tax calculations:

  • The commercial surface mentioned in notarial deeds in Luxembourg is not defined by a single law. In practice, it generally corresponds to SCP plus weighted ancillary areas.
  • For property tax (Grundsteuer), the area considered is usable area including weighted annexes.
  • For registration duties, the area stated in the notarial deed is authoritative. It is therefore crucial that the notary specifies the measurement method used.

Our converter allows switching between definitions and applying appropriate weighting coefficients to obtain a surface consistent with the intended use (transaction, valuation, tax, property management).
seo.faq1Q: Which surface is used to calculate the price per sqm?
seo.faq1A: In Luxembourg, there is no single legal standard for price per sqm. In practice, agencies and property portals use habitable area (excluding cellars and garages) or usable area (including weighted annexes). The Housing Observatory uses usable area for its statistics. When comparing properties, ensure you are using the same surface definition.
seo.faq2Q: Does the Carrez surface exist in Luxembourg?
seo.faq2A: No, the Carrez law is a French legal provision with no equivalent in Luxembourg. There is no legal obligation to include a certified surface measurement in co-ownership sale deeds. However, increasingly, developers engage a sworn surveyor to certify surfaces, and some notaries recommend appending an OAI FC.04 measurement to the deed.
seo.faq3Q: What weighting coefficient for a terrace in Luxembourg?
seo.faq3A: The coefficient varies by criteria: for a covered terrace adjacent to the living room, the standard coefficient is 0.50. For an open terrace of average size (10-20 sqm), generally 0.30 to 0.40. For large penthouse terraces (50+ sqm), a degressive coefficient applies: 0.50 for the first 20 sqm and 0.25 beyond.
seo.faq4Q: Which surface is used for property tax?
seo.faq4A: For Luxembourg property tax (Grundsteuer), the calculation basis is the unit value (Einheitswert) determined by the Direct Tax Administration. This value takes into account usable area including weighted annexes. However, unit values have not been reassessed since 1941, making them largely disconnected from current surfaces and values. A reform has been under discussion for several years.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about construction costs
seo.estimerTitle: Estimating construction costs in Luxembourg in 2026
seo.estimerContent:
Luxembourg ranks among the European countries with the highest construction costs, due to strict regulatory requirements (energy standards, material quality), the cost of skilled labour and pressure on construction companies.

Our estimator relies on data from STATEC (construction cost index), the OAI (Order of Architects and Consulting Engineers) and price schedules from recent tenders. Costs are expressed in EUR/sqm of gross built area (SCB) or net construction area (SCP) depending on the chosen reference standard.

The estimate covers all building trades: structural works, roof, facades, joinery, technical lots (HVAC, electrical, plumbing, sanitary), finishes and external works. Civil engineering (VRD) is treated separately in our dedicated tool.
seo.prixM2Title: Price per sqm by construction type
seo.prixM2Content:
Construction costs in Luxembourg vary significantly by project type (2025 data, excluding land and fees):

  • Standard residential collective: EUR 2,200 to 2,800/sqm SCP. Covers a 10 to 40-unit building with average-quality finishes, meeting AA energy requirements (nZEB-like performance).
  • High-end residential: EUR 3,000 to 3,800/sqm SCP. Superior finishes, home automation, triple glazing, premium materials.
  • Detached house: EUR 2,500 to 4,000/sqm, high variation by area and finish. Passive or positive-energy houses add 10 to 15% surcharge.
  • Offices: EUR 2,000 to 3,200/sqm. Raised floors, suspended ceilings, air conditioning, access control. BREEAM/DGNB certifications add 5 to 8%.
  • Retail: EUR 1,500 to 2,500/sqm. Large open spaces, adapted load-bearing structure, loading docks.
seo.facteursContent:
Several factors affect construction costs in Luxembourg:

  • Location: Luxembourg-based companies are more expensive than those from the Greater Region (Belgium, France, Germany). City-centre sites incur logistics surcharges.
  • Architectural complexity: irregular shapes, large spans, deep basements and green roofs significantly increase costs.
  • Finish level: the gap between standard and high-end finishes can represent 30 to 50% of fit-out cost.
  • Energy standards: since the Grand-Ducal regulation of 9 June 2021, new buildings must meet the nZEB standard. Enhanced insulation, double-flow ventilation, photovoltaic panels and heat pumps represent an 8 to 12% surcharge.
  • Market conditions: material inflation (timber, steel, copper) and skilled labour shortages influence prices cyclically.
seo.normesContent:
In Luxembourg, two surface frameworks coexist and directly impact cost-per-sqm calculations:

  • OAI FC.04: the OAI reference defines Net Construction Area (SCP), Gross Built Area (SCB) and Usable Area (SU). Construction costs are generally expressed in EUR/sqm SCP.
  • ILNAS EN 15221-6: European standard transposed in Luxembourg harmonising surface definitions for facility management.

Confusion between SCB and SCP can lead to 15 to 20% discrepancies in cost estimates. Our tool allows switching between frameworks and integrates standard conversion ratios (SCB/SCP = 1.15 to 1.25 depending on typology).
seo.faq1Q: Should I budget for special foundations?
seo.faq1A: In Luxembourg, special foundations (piles, diaphragm walls, micropiles) are needed in alluvial zones (Alzette valley, Moselle) or sloping terrain. The surcharge ranges from EUR 50 to 200/sqm of footprint. A prior geotechnical study is essential and costs EUR 5,000 to 15,000. We recommend adding a 5 to 8% provision to the foundations line item if no detailed study is available.
seo.faq2Q: What is the surcharge for sustainable and bio-sourced materials?
seo.faq2A: Using bio-sourced materials (CLT solid wood, hemp/wood fibre insulation, lime renders) entails a 10 to 20% surcharge on affected lots versus conventional materials. However, these materials can earn bonuses under LENOZ certification and improve the building's carbon footprint. Timber construction is increasingly competitive in Luxembourg, particularly for 3 to 6-storey buildings.
seo.faq3Q: What are average construction timelines in Luxembourg?
seo.faq3A: Timelines vary by project size: 12 to 18 months for a detached house, 18 to 24 months for a small collective building (10-20 units), 24 to 36 months for a major project (50+ units or office building). These timelines exclude design and permit phases which can add 12 to 24 months. A 15 to 20% schedule contingency is prudent.
seo.faq4A: Architect fees in Luxembourg are governed by OAI regulations and calculated as a percentage of construction cost. For a residential project, expect 8 to 12% of construction cost (excl. VAT). This covers sketch, preliminary design, permit application, execution documents, tendering, construction supervision and handover. Engineering consultant fees add 3 to 5% on top.

calculateurVrd.title: VRD Estimator — Engineering Office
calculateurVrd.subtitle: Quantities, budget estimate and summary schedule — Luxembourg infrastructure projects

steps.stepNote0: APS phase — Define the project scope: type of operation, footprint, municipality. These parameters determine applicable standards (municipal vs state roads, CTG 002/009).
steps.stepNote1: Lot 0-1 — Site installation and earthmoving. Earthworks typically represent 10-15% of the VRD budget. Ref.: CTG 002 (earthworks), CSDC-CT §2 (subgrade layer).
steps.stepNote2: Lot 2-3 — Pavement structure and kerbs. The structure is defined by the CSDC-CT (Ponts & Chaussées). Typical thicknesses: 6 cm BB + 5 cm GB + 20 cm GNT + 30 cm foundation.
steps.stepNote3: Lots 4-5 — WW/RW networks per CTG 009 (piping). WW diameters: DN200-400, RW: DN300-600. Retention basin mandatory for projects >0.5 ha (RGD drainage).
steps.stepNote4: Lot 6 — Combined trench (elec/telecom/gas) + street lighting. Ref.: Creos connection catalogue, POST Technologies, municipal technical regulations.
steps.stepNote5: Lots 7-8 — Green spaces, signage, street furniture. Batiprix 2026 vol. 2 for reference prices.
steps.stepNote6: Fees and ancillary studies. APS/APD/DCE phases per OAI CTA Privé. Engineering fees: 8-12% typical, free negotiation (Competition Council decision 2024).

hints.prixDecapage: 3-8 €/m² -- Source: Batiprix 2026 vol. 2 (x1.20 LU coeff.), CTG 002 art. 3
hints.prixDeblais: 8-25 €/m³ -- Source: Batiprix 2026, STATEC earthworks index Oct. 2025 (-0.6%)
hints.prixRegardEU: 800-2,000 €/u -- Source: Batiprix 2026, Administration de l'eau TabAVIS-9100

hints.etudeHydro: Source: Administration de l'eau, RGD drainage
hints.honorairesBE: Source: OAI CTA Privé annex 3 (indicative, free negotiation since 2024)

sources.title: Sources:
sources.text: Average unit prices on the Luxembourg market 2026. References: Batiprix 2026 vol. 2 (VRD, Green spaces) with LU coefficient x1.20, CTG 002 (earthworks), CTG 009 (piping), CSDC-CT (Ponts & Chaussées), STATEC construction price indices, Creos connection catalogue 2026, OAI CTA Privé annex 3. Prices generally include supply, transport and installation. Detailed quotes should be obtained from specialist contractors.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about civil engineering (VRD) in Luxembourg
seo.viabilisationContent:
Site servicing in Luxembourg encompasses all roadworks and utilities (VRD — Voiries et Réseaux Divers) needed to make a plot buildable and connectable to public networks. These works represent a major budget item in any development or property promotion project.

In Luxembourg, VRD costs are influenced by topography (Gutland plateau vs Oesling hills), soil conditions, distance to existing networks and the specific requirements of each municipality. Luxembourg municipalities have significant autonomy in planning matters, leading to considerable differences in technical specifications.

The Pacte Logement 2.0 (law of 30 July 2021) and the subdivision tax reform have modified infrastructure financing mechanisms, with increased developer participation in public infrastructure costs.
seo.postesContent:
VRD works break down into several main categories:

  • Roadworks: earthworks, subbase, base course, wearing course (asphalt), kerbs, pavements, signage. Average cost: EUR 80 to 150/sqm of road surface. Municipal roads must comply with the Roads and Bridges Administration standards.
  • Water supply networks: pipelines, connections, stop valves, meters. Connection to the municipal network is subject to an infrastructure tax set by municipal regulation (EUR 1,500 to 5,000 per plot).
  • Drainage: separate system (foul water + rainwater) mandatory in new development zones. Retention basins required under the amended law of 19 December 2008 on water.
  • Dry utilities: electricity (underground network mandatory in urban areas), telecommunications (fibre optic), natural gas (Enovos/Creos). Shared trenches optimise costs.
  • Street lighting: poles, LED luminaires, cabling, control cabinets. New installations must comply with the Grand-Ducal regulation of 11 November 2022 on light pollution.
seo.coutParLotTitle: Servicing cost per plot by density
seo.coutParLotContent:
VRD cost per plot varies significantly by development density:

  • Detached houses (5-10 plots/ha): EUR 40,000 to 70,000/plot. Low density means longer network runs per plot.
  • Terraced houses (15-25 plots/ha): EUR 25,000 to 45,000/plot. Intermediate density optimises the network length/plot count ratio.
  • Apartment buildings (50-100 dwellings/ha): EUR 10,000 to 25,000/dwelling. High density pools infrastructure costs.

These ranges include primary VRD (site access) and secondary VRD (internal distribution), but exclude tertiary VRD (private facilities, parking, private landscaping).

The LISER report on land development highlights that densification is the most effective strategy for reducing infrastructure cost per dwelling.
seo.obligationsContent:
In Luxembourg, VRD obligations are governed by several texts:

  • Law of 19 July 2004 (municipal planning): defines buildable zones in the PAG and development conditions in PAPs. The developer is generally required to complete internal VRD and transfer them to the municipality after acceptance.
  • Financial contribution: the developer must contribute to public infrastructure financing (schools, green spaces, transport infrastructure). The Pacte Logement 2.0 provides for convention mechanisms between municipalities and developers.
  • Subdivision agreement: contractual document between developer and municipality setting out VRD technical specifications, completion schedule, financial guarantees (bank guarantee) and transfer conditions.
  • Infrastructure tax: each municipality sets network connection tax amounts by regulation. These taxes are added to VRD work costs.
seo.faq1Q: Who pays for VRD works in a subdivision?
seo.faq1A: In a Luxembourg subdivision, the developer generally funds all internal VRD (internal roads, distribution networks, lighting, green spaces) and contributes to external VRD (upstream network reinforcement). After acceptance, roads and networks are transferred to the municipality for maintenance. VRD costs are passed on in the sale price of plots or dwellings.
seo.faq2Q: What is the cost of connecting to public networks?
seo.faq2A: The cost depends on the municipality and network type. Water supply: EUR 1,500 to 5,000 per plot. Drainage: EUR 2,000 to 8,000. Electrical connection (Creos): EUR 2,000 to 6,000. Fibre optic (POST Luxembourg) is generally free for new construction. Total connection fees represent EUR 8,000 to 20,000 per plot.
seo.faq3Q: How to manage a sloping site for VRD?
seo.faq3A: A sloping site in Luxembourg (common in the Oesling and valleys) entails significant surcharges: retaining walls (EUR 150 to 500/sqm of facing), major earthworks, specific drainage systems, and pressure pipelines. The overall surcharge can reach 30 to 50% compared to a flat site. A prior topographic and geotechnical survey is essential.
seo.faq4Q: What is the average site servicing timeline?
seo.faq4A: Site servicing typically takes 6 to 12 months for the works themselves, excluding administrative timelines. Preparatory phases (studies, municipal approvals) can add 6 to 18 months. Provisional acceptance occurs at work completion, followed by a 1-year warranty period before final acceptance and transfer to the municipality. The total timeline is typically 18 to 36 months.

hvac.title: HVAC Simulator - Sizing and costing
hvac.subtitle: Sizing, product selection and budget estimation for heating, ventilation and air conditioning installations in Luxembourg.

steps.stepNote0: Step 1 - Building characteristics. These parameters determine thermal heat losses according to EN 12831. The design outdoor temperature ranges from -10 C (Luxembourg City) to -12 C (Ardennes).
steps.stepNote1: Simplified calculation of thermal heat losses according to EN 12831. The total required capacity includes heating and domestic hot water (DHW) production.
steps.stepNote2: Selection of the heating system from a catalogue of products available in Luxembourg. Products whose power range covers your requirement are marked Recommended.
steps.stepNote3: Sizing of heat emitters (underfloor heating, radiators) and hydraulic equipment (cylinders, circulators, valves). Price sources: Rehau, Uponor, Kermi, Purmo.
steps.stepNote4: Mechanical ventilation with heat recovery (MVHR). A double-flow MVHR with heat recovery is strongly recommended for energy renovations in Luxembourg (RGD 9 June 2021).
steps.stepNote5: Costing summary and calculation of financial aids (Klimabonus 2026, Enoprimes, VAT 3%). Enable fossil fuel replacement to maximise aids.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about HVAC sizing
seo.dimensionnementContent: HVAC sizing uses the EN 12831 standard adapted to Luxembourg's climate (design temperature -12 to -15 degrees C). Heating needs range from 25 kWh/m2/year (class A) to 300+ kWh/m2/year (class I).
seo.normesTitle: EN 12831 standard and Luxembourg climate conditions
seo.normesContent: Transmission and ventilation losses calculation, design temperature -12 degrees C for Luxembourg City, -15 degrees C for highland areas.
seo.pacTitle: Heat pump vs. pellets: comparison for Luxembourg
seo.pacContent: Heat pump (COP 3.0-4.0, EUR 15,000-22,000) for well-insulated buildings (A-D). Pellet boiler (85-95% efficiency, EUR 12,000-18,000) for houses with storage space. Hybrid recommended for E-I classes.
seo.vmcTitle: Heat recovery ventilation: essential for high-performance buildings
seo.vmcContent: Dual-flow HRV recovers 80-95% of exhaust heat. Cost EUR 5,000-12,000, saves 25-40% of heating needs.
seo.faq1q: How do I size my heating system?
seo.faq1a: Based on EN 12831: transmission and ventilation losses, considering surface area, insulation and Luxembourg design temperatures (-12 to -15 degrees C).
seo.faq2q: Heat pump or pellets for Luxembourg?
seo.faq2a: Heat pump for well-insulated homes (A-D), pellets for older houses with storage. Hybrid recommended for E-I.
seo.faq3q: Is HRV ventilation mandatory?
seo.faq3a: Not formally, but practically indispensable for classes A-B.
seo.faq4q: What budget for a complete HVAC system?
seo.faq4a: For 150m2: heat pump (~EUR 18,000) + HRV (~EUR 8,000) + distribution (~EUR 5,000) = ~EUR 30,000-35,000 before Klimabonus.
seo.faq5a: Insulate first, then right-size the system. Over-sizing reduces heat pump COP. Heating curve adjustment is essential.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about real estate portfolio management
seo.gestionTitle: Managing a real estate portfolio in Luxembourg
seo.gestionContent:
Real estate portfolio management in Luxembourg requires a structured approach integrating financial, tax, energy and legal dimensions. Whether you hold 2 or 20 properties, systematic monitoring of performance indicators is essential to optimise returns and anticipate risks.

The Luxembourg real estate market has specificities impacting portfolio management: favourable taxation of rental income (interest deduction, accelerated depreciation under conditions), a strict regulatory framework for residential leases (law of 21 September 2006), and increasingly stringent energy legislation (EPBD directive, CPE obligations).

Our Portfolio tool consolidates all your tevaxia valuations and calculations to provide a unified view of your assets: total value, aggregate yield, interest rate exposure, geographic distribution and energy performance.
seo.indicateursContent:
Essential performance indicators for managing your real estate portfolio:

  • Gross yield: ratio between annual rent collected and the property's market value. In Luxembourg, gross residential yields range from 3.0% to 4.5% (2025). Offices offer yields of 4.0 to 5.5%.
  • Net yield: gross yield less non-recoverable charges, vacancy, maintenance, insurance and property tax. Net yield is typically 1.0 to 1.5 points below gross yield.
  • Portfolio LTV ratio: total debt relative to total portfolio value. An LTV above 60% is considered risky. Banks monitor this ratio for multi-property investors.
  • Geographic allocation: concentration in a single municipality exposes you to idiosyncratic risk. Diversification between Luxembourg City, the Southern Belt and the North reduces volatility.
  • Asset type allocation: mixing residential and commercial benefits from different economic cycles.
seo.performanceContent:
Time-series performance tracking enables trend identification and informed decision-making:

  • Value evolution: comparison of portfolio value at year N vs N-1, broken down between market effect (price changes) and scope effect (acquisitions/disposals).
  • Rent evolution: tracking annual indexations (IPCN for residential leases, agreed indices for commercial leases) and rebasements at renewal.
  • Vacancy: actual vacancy rate compared to market structural vacancy. A rate above average may signal a positioning issue.
  • CAPEX and works: tracking maintenance and improvement investments. The norm is to provision 1 to 2% of property value per year for upkeep.
seo.energieTitle: Weighted CPE energy score of the property portfolio
seo.energieContent:
Your portfolio's energy performance has become a major strategic issue in Luxembourg:

  • EPBD Directive: the 2024 revision imposes ambitious renovation trajectories. The most energy-intensive buildings (classes F-G) must reach class E by 2030 and class D by 2033.
  • Weighted CPE score: our tool calculates an average energy score for your portfolio, weighted by each property's area. This score helps identify energy sieves and prioritise renovation investments.
  • Green premium / brown discount: in Luxembourg, A-B rated properties benefit from a 5 to 8% value premium. F-G rated properties suffer an 8 to 15% discount and more restrictive financing conditions.
  • Renovation subsidies: the PRIMe House scheme offers generous grants (insulation, heating, renewables) covering 30 to 50% of energy renovation costs.
seo.faq1Q: How to diversify a real estate portfolio in Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: Diversification can operate across several axes: geographic (Luxembourg City vs Southern Belt vs North), typological (residential vs offices vs retail), lease type (residential vs commercial leases), and temporal (new vs existing properties). A balanced portfolio might comprise 60-70% residential (stable income) and 30-40% commercial (higher yields).
seo.faq2Q: What yield to target for a buy-to-let investment in Luxembourg?
seo.faq2A: Target yields vary by strategy. For core residential (new, well-located), target a net yield of 2.5 to 3.5%. For value-add residential (renovation needed), the post-works net yield target is 3.5 to 4.5%. For prime offices in Luxembourg City, net yields are 3.5 to 4.5%. Always compare with alternative investments (life insurance, bonds).
seo.faq3Q: What is the tax treatment of a multi-property portfolio in Luxembourg?
seo.faq3A: Rental income is taxed at the progressive rate (marginal rate of 42% + 9% employment fund contribution). Deductible charges include loan interest, maintenance costs, insurance, property tax and depreciation (2% per year). Capital gains on disposal benefit from a reduced rate after 2 years of ownership. Holding via an SCI or corporate vehicle may offer specific advantages.
seo.faq4Q: When should I sell a property from my portfolio?
seo.faq4A: Sell signals include: net yield below 2% with no improvement prospect, major works ahead, CPE class F or G with disproportionate renovation cost, changing neighbourhood dynamics, or a more attractive reinvestment opportunity. Tax-wise, it is preferable to sell after 2 years of ownership to benefit from the reduced capital gains rate.

portfolio.cashflowSubtitle: Monthly net rental income, with mild LU seasonality (Dec -10 %, summer +5 %). Assumptions: 5 % vacancy, 15 % non-recoverable costs, interest-only debt service at 3.5 %.
portfolio.cashflowNote: Indicative estimate. For a precise forecast, use /gestion-locative with actual rents and bank amortization schedule.
portfolio.expensesSubtitle: Annual operating expenses estimate per property and type (condo, insurance, property tax, maintenance, management).
portfolio.expensesNote: Indicative assumptions: condo 250 €/mo, PNO insurance 0.5%/y of value, property tax 0.1%, maintenance 0.5%, management 5% of rent. Expense/rent ratio ≤ 25% healthy, ≥ 35% concerning.

A coefficient out of range is not necessarily wrong — it may reflect a recent LU specificity (e.g. stronger brown discount since 2023).
hedonique.source_disclaimer: Coefficients calibrated on Observatoire de l'Habitat data and Spuerkeess publications. This is not the official Observatory model (unpublished) but an approximation based on published results.
hedonique.select_commune_prompt: Select a municipality to get the hedonic estimation

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about the hedonic model
seo.modeleTitle: The hedonic model applied to the Luxembourg market
seo.modeleContent:
The hedonic model is an econometric approach that decomposes a property's price into the sum of implicit values of its characteristics. Developed by Rosen (1974), it rests on the hypothesis that the market price reflects buyers' preferences for each housing attribute.

In Luxembourg, the Housing Observatory (Ministry of Housing / LISER) regularly publishes hedonic models calibrated on actual transactions recorded by the Registration Administration (AED). These models enable construction of quality-constant price indices, neutralising the effect of changes in the composition of properties sold.

Our implementation uses a log-linear model: ln(Price) = β0 + Σ(βi × Xi) + ε. The dependent variable is the log of price, allowing coefficients to be interpreted directly as elasticities (percentage impact). Explanatory variables cover area, location (municipality/neighbourhood), floor, general condition, CPE energy class, parking availability and outdoor amenities.
seo.coefficientsContent:
Our model coefficients are calibrated from available Luxembourg data:

  • Area: elasticity of -0.35% per additional sqm above 80 sqm. This coefficient reflects the declining marginal price per sqm with increasing area — a well-documented phenomenon in property literature.
  • Floor: ground floor suffers a discount of approximately 7%, first floor 3%. Upper floors (4+) benefit from a 3% premium, and penthouses up to 8%. Source: Housing Observatory.
  • General condition: a new or fully renovated property earns a premium of approximately 7%. A property needing cosmetic work suffers a 5% discount, and one needing full renovation 15%.
  • CPE energy class: classes A-B bring a 5% premium, while F-G carry an 8% discount. Spuerkeess has documented this gap in its Luxembourg green premium studies.
  • Parking: an indoor parking space adds approximately 5% to the dwelling price (equivalent to EUR 30,000-45,000 depending on location).
seo.sourcesContent:
The reliability of a hedonic model depends directly on the quality and representativeness of underlying data. In Luxembourg, we use several sources:

  • Housing Observatory: hedonic price indices published quarterly, based on notarial deeds transmitted by the AED. Comprehensive coverage of the residential market since 2010.
  • STATEC: housing price index (IPIL), average prices by municipality, construction statistics.
  • Municipal authorities: PAG/PAP data, building permits, cadastral information.
  • Property listings: asking prices serve as a supplementary reference but are generally 5 to 10% above actual transaction prices.

Coefficient updates are performed semi-annually to incorporate market developments.
seo.limitesTitle: Model limitations: R² and confidence interval
seo.limitesContent:
Every statistical model has limitations that must be understood:

  • R² (coefficient of determination): our model shows an R² of approximately 0.75, meaning 75% of price variance is explained by the retained variables. The remaining 25% relates to unobserved factors (view quality, natural light, noise, micro-location, common area condition).
  • Confidence interval: the point estimate is always accompanied by an interval. In dense urban areas with many transactions, the interval is ±10%. In rural areas with limited data, it can reach ±18%.
  • Atypical properties: the model is calibrated on standard properties. Luxury properties, character houses or properties with major defects may deviate significantly from the estimate.
  • Temporal effect: coefficients reflect market conditions at calibration time. During rapid market shifts, a lag may appear.
seo.faq1Q: What is the R² of the hedonic model for Luxembourg?
seo.faq1A: Our model's R² is approximately 0.75, comparable to results obtained by the Housing Observatory in its official publications. This means 75% of transaction price variance is explained by observed property characteristics (area, location, floor, condition, energy). The remaining 25% depends on unmeasurable factors such as view, noise or finish condition.
seo.faq2Q: What is the difference between a hedonic model and a property appraisal?
seo.faq2A: The hedonic model provides a statistical estimate based on market data, without a physical property visit. A property appraisal (EVS-compliant) includes an on-site inspection, thorough legal analysis (easements, planning), verification of actual condition and professional judgement. The hedonic model is ideal for initial price framing or a quick opinion of value, while an appraisal is necessary for banking operations, successions or disputes.
seo.faq3Q: Is the hedonic model applicable to commercial real estate?
seo.faq3A: Our hedonic model is calibrated primarily on the Luxembourg residential market (apartments and houses). For commercial real estate (offices, retail, logistics), income-based valuation methods (capitalisation, DCF) are more appropriate as the price depends essentially on rental yield. We offer a dedicated multi-tenant DCF tool for investment property.
seo.faq4Q: How accurate is the model by municipality?
seo.faq4A: Accuracy varies by transaction density. In Luxembourg City, Esch-sur-Alzette or Differdange, where volumes are high, the confidence interval is ±10%. In northern rural municipalities (Winseler, Kiischpelt) with fewer than 10 transactions per year, the interval can reach ±18%. The model systematically displays a confidence index adapted to each municipality.

indices.title: tevaxia Index — March 2026
indices.subtitle: Monthly property price index by municipality in Luxembourg
indices.analysis_paragraph_1: In March 2026, the Luxembourg market shows signs of recovery after the 2022–2023 correction. The national average price stands at <price></price>, up 2.1% year-on-year. Municipalities in the Luxembourg canton remain the most expensive, but the most dynamic increases are in developing secondary hubs.
indices.analysis_paragraph_2: The transaction volume ({transactions} over the last quarter) confirms a return of liquidity, after the trough observed in 2023. Interest rates, having reached their peak, are stabilising, which supports demand.
indices.source: Source: Observatoire de l'Habitat (data.public.lu), STATEC. Indicative data, updated quarterly. Last update: March 2026.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about price indices
seo.indiceTitle: Property price indices by municipality in Luxembourg
seo.indiceContent:
The property price index by municipality is the reference tool for tracking the Luxembourg residential market. Published quarterly by the Housing Observatory in partnership with STATEC, it relies on notarial deeds registered with the AED.

In 2025, the overall index shows stabilisation after the 2022-2024 correction (-14% on average), with marked disparities between municipalities.
seo.methodologieContent:
The methodology relies on:

  • Primary source: notarial deeds registered at the AED
  • Hedonic method: price decomposition by property characteristics
  • Seasonal adjustment: X-13 ARIMA model used by STATEC
  • Smoothing: 4-quarter moving average for low-volume municipalities
seo.tendancesContent:
Municipal analysis reveals contrasting dynamics:

  • Luxembourg City: median apartment ~EUR 10,500/m2, +3% recovery in Q1-2025
  • Southern belt (Esch, Differdange): stabilisation at ~EUR 5,800-6,500/m2
  • North (Clervaux, Wiltz): slower correction, ~EUR 4,500-5,500/m2
seo.topCommunesTitle: Top municipalities: prices, yields and attractiveness
seo.topCommunesContent:
The most sought-after municipalities combine transport accessibility, quality of life and school offering:

  • Strassen, Bertrange, Hesperange: premium suburban, EUR 8,000-10,000/m2
  • Esch-sur-Alzette, Belval: secondary urban hub, ~EUR 6,000/m2
  • Mersch, Steinsel: attractive central municipalities, EUR 6,500-7,500/m2
seo.faq1q: Where do the price data per municipality come from?
seo.faq1a: Data comes from notarial deeds registered with the AED, aggregated by the Housing Observatory. These are actual transaction prices, not advertised prices.
seo.faq2q: How often are the indices updated?
seo.faq2a: Official indices are published quarterly with a 2-3 month lag. Our tool integrates the latest available data.
seo.faq3q: Why do prices differ between neighbouring municipalities?
seo.faq3a: Differences are explained by location (proximity to employment, transport), school offering, municipal taxation, and available property types.
seo.faq4q: Do the indices include new-build and existing properties?
seo.faq4a: Yes, both segments are covered. The Observatory also publishes separate indices for new (VEFA) and existing apartments.
seo.faq5q: How can I use the indices to estimate my property?
seo.faq5a: Indices provide a median price per municipality as a starting point. Then adjust for your property's specific characteristics using our estimation and comparison tools.

pagPap.title: Urban Planning — PAG / PAP
pagPap.subtitle: Search by municipality, zones, buildability, procedures
pagPap.searchAddressHint: Enter an address (street + number + locality) to see the exact PAG zone on Geoportail.
pagPap.searchAddressNote: Geoportail opens the official PAG map centred on the address. Click on a parcel to see the applicable zone.
pagPap.searchAddressNoResult: No address found. Try a more complete label (number + street + locality).

pagPap.ctvSanction: Penalty: automatic reclassification as non-buildable zone.
pagPap.ctlDescription: 3-year deadline after servicing to begin construction works.

pagPap.valueImpactText: Land nearing CTV/CTL expiration is subject to a significant discount. Always check remaining deadlines.
pagPap.geoportailSourceNote: Source: Geoportail.lu (ACT) — Digital cadastral plan, PAG layer. Indicative data.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about PAG and PAP
seo.urbanismeTitle: Urban planning in Luxembourg: PAG and PAP
seo.urbanismeContent: Luxembourg's land planning is governed by the amended law of 19 July 2004. Each municipality has a General Development Plan (PAG) defining land use, and Particular Development Plans (PAP) detailing rules per zone.
seo.zonesTitle: Zones and buildability: understanding the rules
seo.zonesContent: Main buildable zones: HAB-1 (low density), HAB-2 (medium density), MIX (mixed use). COS and CUS coefficients define building intensity.
seo.constructibiliteContent: Assessment involves: PAG classification check, PAP rules analysis, easement verification, and feasibility study (utilities, road access).
seo.servitudesContent: Main easements: urban planning (setbacks, protected sectors), environmental (Natura 2000, flood zones), heritage (listed buildings), and private (rights of way).
seo.faq1q: Where can I view my municipality's PAG?
seo.faq1a: The PAG is available online at the Geoportail (geoportail.lu), at the town hall, or through the municipality's planning department.
seo.faq2q: What is the difference between PAG and PAP?
seo.faq2a: The PAG defines general zone allocations across the entire municipal territory. The PAP details construction rules for each specific zone or sector.
seo.faq3q: How do I know if land is buildable?
seo.faq3a: Check the PAG classification, applicable PAP rules, easements and utility availability.
seo.faq4q: What are COS and CUS?
seo.faq4a: COS is the ground coverage ratio, CUS is the total floor area ratio. They determine allowed building intensity.

amlKyc.title: AML / KYC Real Estate
amlKyc.subtitle: Anti-money laundering checklist for real estate transactions in Luxembourg — Law of 12 November 2004, as amended
amlKyc.propertyAddressPlaceholder: E.g.: 12 rue de la Gare, L-1234 Luxembourg
amlKyc.sanctionSearchDesc: Check whether a person or entity appears on the European Union sanctions map (sanctionsmap.eu).

amlKyc.id2Desc: Utility bill less than 3 months old or residence certificate
amlKyc.id3Desc: If legal entity: RBE register, organisation chart, articles of association

amlKyc.vig2Desc: Check whether the client or funds originate from a high-risk country (FATF list)
amlKyc.vig3Desc: If acquisition via SCI/SPV/holding: analyse the ownership chain
amlKyc.vig4Desc: Price significantly above/below market, cash payment, unjustified urgency

amlKyc.doc2Desc: Bank loan offer or proof of own funds

amlKyc.decl3Desc: Name of the AML compliance officer within the organisation

amlKyc.ref3Desc: on AML obligations for financial sector professionals.

seo.faqTitle: Frequently asked questions about real estate AML/KYC
seo.obligationsTitle: Anti-money laundering obligations in Luxembourg real estate
seo.obligationsContent:
Anti-money laundering (AML) is a major obligation for all real estate professionals in Luxembourg. The real estate sector is identified by FATF (Financial Action Task Force) as a particularly high-risk money laundering vector due to high transaction amounts and complex ownership structures.

In Luxembourg, real estate professionals (agents, developers, property managers, valuers) have been subject to due diligence obligations since the transposition of the 4th EU AML Directive. The CSSF and the Ministry of Justice provide supervision.

Penalties for non-compliance are severe: administrative fines up to EUR 5 million or 10% of turnover, criminal sanctions (1 to 5 years imprisonment), practice bans and considerable reputational damage.
seo.loi2004Content:
The law of 12 November 2004 on combating money laundering and terrorist financing, amended several times (notably in 2018 and 2020 to transpose the 4th and 5th AML Directives), constitutes the reference legal framework:

  • Scope: any natural or legal person who, in a professional capacity, is involved in transactions relating to the purchase, sale, rental or management of real estate.
  • Main obligations: identification and verification of client and beneficial owner identity, risk assessment and management, document retention, staff training, suspicious activity reporting to the FIU.
  • Risk-based approach: enhanced due diligence measures for high-risk clients (PEPs, high-risk countries, complex structures).
  • Beneficial ownership register (RBE): companies holding real estate must register their beneficial owners with the RBE managed by Luxembourg Business Registers.
seo.identificationTitle: Client identification and enhanced due diligence
seo.identificationContent:
Identification and due diligence (KYC — Know Your Customer) measures in the Luxembourg real estate sector comprise:

  • Standard identification: identity verification (official ID, passport), address verification (utility bill, bank certificate) and source of funds verification. For legal entities: articles of association, RCS extract, identification of shareholders and beneficial owners (25% threshold).
  • Enhanced Due Diligence: mandatory for politically exposed persons (PEPs), clients resident in high-risk countries (FATF list), unusually complex or high-value transactions, and opaque ownership structures.
  • Simplified due diligence: possible for clients introduced by a fellow obliged entity, Luxembourg public institutions or companies listed on a regulated EU market.
  • Ongoing monitoring: the due diligence obligation is not limited to onboarding. The professional must regularly update KYC information and monitor transactions.
seo.declarationContent:
The reporting and sanctions framework is a pillar of the Luxembourg AML system:

  • Suspicious activity report (SAR): any professional who suspects a transaction linked to money laundering or terrorist financing must file a report with the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) of the Luxembourg Public Prosecutor's Office. The report should be filed before executing the transaction if possible.
  • Tipping-off: it is strictly prohibited to inform the client or any third party that a report has been filed (Article 5-1 of the law).
  • Administrative sanctions: fines of EUR 1,250 to 5,000,000 for legal entities. The Ministry of Justice may issue a reprimand, temporary practice ban or licence withdrawal.
  • Criminal sanctions: money laundering carries 1 to 5 years' imprisonment and a fine of EUR 1,250 to 1,250,000 (Article 506-1 of the Criminal Code). Failure to report is also criminally punishable.
  • Sanctions register: administrative sanctions are published on the Ministry of Justice website.
seo.faq1Q: Who is subject to AML obligations in Luxembourg real estate?
seo.faq1A: Subject entities include: estate agents (registered with the trade register), property developers, property managers, co-ownership syndics, notaries (for real estate transactions), lawyers (when assisting clients with property transactions), and property valuation experts. Any person professionally involved in a real estate transaction is potentially subject.
seo.faq2Q: How to identify a Politically Exposed Person (PEP)?
seo.faq2A: A PEP is a person holding or having held a prominent political, judicial or administrative function: heads of state, ministers, parliamentarians, senior judges, ambassadors, senior military officers, leaders of state-owned enterprises. Close family members and known close associates are also considered PEPs. Specialist databases (World-Check, Dow Jones) enable systematic screening.
seo.faq3Q: Is there a reporting threshold for real estate transactions?
seo.faq3A: There is no monetary threshold below which due diligence obligations do not apply. Every real estate transaction, regardless of amount, is subject to KYC obligations. However, the suspicious activity report is based on qualitative indicators (unusual client behaviour, inconsistency between profile and transaction, use of complex structures) rather than a quantitative threshold. Cash payments above EUR 10,000 are subject to a specific reporting obligation.
seo.faq4Q: How long must KYC documents be retained?
seo.faq4A: The law of 12 November 2004 requires KYC documents and information to be retained for a minimum of 5 years after the end of the business relationship or the date of the occasional transaction. Transaction documents must be kept for 5 years after execution. In practice, given Luxembourg criminal limitation periods (5 to 10 years), it is recommended to retain documents for 10 years.

amlKyc.sanctionOfficialNote: Mandatory verification for politically exposed persons (PEP), sanctioned entities, and beneficial owners. Keep proof of consultation with timestamp in client file (5-year obligation art. 3 law 12/11/2004).

terresAgricoles.title: Valuation — Agricultural Land
terresAgricoles.subtitle: Estimating the value of agricultural land and buildings in Luxembourg
terresAgricoles.ruralLeaseHint: ~30% discount — occupancy, lower liquidity, tenant rights
